coredata.dc:title: In operando and in situ characterization tools for advanced rechargeable batteries: Effects of electrode origin and electrolyte
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.

coredata.dc:creator: Dorri, Mehrdad
M R, Anil Kumar
Zaghib, Karim
coredata.dc:description: Understanding the intricate interactions between electrodes and electrolytes during battery operation is critical for developing high-performance, long-lasting, and safe rechargeable batteries. Structural and chemical processes occurring within battery materials in real time can now be monitored using in situ and operando characterization techniques. In situ and operando electron microscopy and X-ray-based techniques are valuable because they directly reveal reaction pathways and degradation processes with high resolution across multiple length scales. This review first provides a detailed overview of these techniques, including the historical development of experimental setups, their working principles, and the specialized cell designs created for battery research. Recent studies are then discussed with a focus on Li-ion, Na-ion, all-solid-state, and aqueous battery systems. Special attention is given to how these techniques uncover ion insertion and transport processes, interfacial and side reactions, mechanical degradation, dendrite growth, and failure mechanisms. Ongoing efforts are expected in employing in situ and operando approaches to address critical research challenges, such as reaction inhomogeneity in commercial cathodes, fast charging, safe and reversible Li metal plating, and solid electrolyte interphase stabilization. A deeper understanding of component behavior across different battery chemistries is essential to improving current technologies and developing next-generation energy storage systems.

Capability (analytical focus)
Advantages
Limitations
Ref
∼μm
Phase evolution, SEI composition, crystal structure changes, Li concentration gradients and mechanical analysis
Easy to use, non-destructive, quantitative analysis, highly penetrative even through case, and versatility
Insensitive to amorphous or disordered components, overlapping diffraction from cell parts,
∼μm
Surface-sensitive chemical analysis, elemental composition, SEI composition and oxidation states
Penetration depth is nanometers scale, observing transient phases in the SEI
Require high vacuum, operando with liquid electrolytes is challenging, no bulk information, data acquisition is relatively slow
Elemental composition, phase transformations, XANES yields oxidation state and electronic structure; EXAFS provides interatomic distances and coordination numbers
Probes short-range order in both crystalline and amorphous materials.
Requires synchrotron source and careful calibration.
≥50 nm
High-resolution 2D/3D electrode imaging, can directly visualize dendrite nucleation and growth
Non-destructive, and high spatial/temporal resolution
Complicated experimental setup
≥30 nm
Elemental imaging and mapping the concentration (with XAS)
non-destructive, capability to combine with SEM
Low resolution for lab-scale instruments, and sample preparation
50 nm
3D internal structure, morphology quantification, and dendrite growth dynamics
Non-destructive, quantitative analysis (combined with AI and ML techniques)
Low resolution based on the type of CT system, challenging experimental setup for CT systems.
10 nm
Morphology evolution, high-resolution surface imaging, elemental analysis (with EDS)
Large depth of field; easy to use, data acquisition is quick
High vacuum environment, coating for insulators, and beam damage
0.1 nm
Morphology evolution, atomic-level structure analysis, and can visualize SEI formation and dendrite growth at the nanoscale
Highest resolution, chemical information (with ES/EELS)
High vacuum environment; ultra-thin samples, and beam damage
10 nm
Quantitative Li concentration, isotope-specific depth profiling, and structural information of electrode
Non-destructive, Li distribution, penetrating capability in real cells
Vacuum/pressure condition; high cost, near-surface only
∼μm
2D/3D images, Li distribution
Non-destructive, highly sensitive to light elements like hydrogen and lithium, useful for studying large-scale cells
High cost, lower resolution compared to other microscopy techniques
∼1 μm
Molecular species and functional groups, detects electrolyte, electrode and SEI species
Non-destructive, fast and easy to use
Fluorescence interference
∼μm
Molecular information, chemical composition, SEI formation, and functional groups in aqueous batteries
Non-destructive, fast and easy to use, continuous concentration measurements of gases
Fluorescence interference, measuring inorganic compounds
200 nm
Morphology evolution, Li dendrite, interfacial changes, gas bubble formation
fast and easy to use, low cost, non-vacuum condition
Low resolution, might miss micro-scale SEI or cracks until they become large
10 nm
Topographical mapping of electrode surfaces, mechanical properties, interface stability, and detect thick SEI formation
High resolution, 3D surface profiling, wide range of materials, coupled with other techniques
Slow scanning, AFM in a liquid electrolyte can introduce noise and drift
100 nm
Morphology evolution, interfaces of electrodes and electrolytes, rate and chemical detection
kinetic and electrochemical data in high resolution
Tip size dependent, and near-surface

head.ce:title: In operando and in situ characterization tools for advanced rechargeable batteries: Effects of electrode origin and electrolyte

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Concordia University, 1455 De Maisonneuve Blvd. West, Montreal, Quebec, H3G 1M8, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering, Concordia University, 1455 De Maisonneuve Blvd. West, Montreal, Quebec H3G 1M8, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Understanding the intricate interactions between electrodes and electrolytes during battery operation is critical for developing high-performance, long-lasting, and safe rechargeable batteries. Structural and chemical processes occurring within battery materials in real time can now be monitored using in situ and operando characterization techniques. In situ and operando electron microscopy and X-ray-based techniques are valuable because they directly reveal reaction pathways and degradation processes with high resolution across multiple length scales. This review first provides a detailed overview of these techniques, including the historical development of experimental setups, their working principles, and the specialized cell designs created for battery research. Recent studies are then discussed with a focus on Li-ion, Na-ion, all-solid-state, and aqueous battery systems. Special attention is given to how these techniques uncover ion insertion and transport processes, interfacial and side reactions, mechanical degradation, dendrite growth, and failure mechanisms. Ongoing efforts are expected in employing in situ and operando approaches to address critical research challenges, such as reaction inhomogeneity in commercial cathodes, fast charging, safe and reversible Li metal plating, and solid electrolyte interphase stabilization. A deeper understanding of component behavior across different battery chemistries is essential to improving current technologies and developing next-generation energy storage systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: In the modern world, a notable increase in energy demand, coupled with environmental concerns, has prompted researchers to explore various renewable energy alternatives such as wind, solar, and tidal energy [1,2]. This shift has gained momentum after the Paris Agreement, when countries made a global commitment to transition from conventional fossil fuels to environmentally friendly renewable energy sources [3,4]. Electrochemical devices, particularly rechargeable batteries, play a crucial role in such transitions by efficiently capturing and storing renewable energy for later use [5–7]. These battery systems are already employed in consumer electronics, electric vehicles, large-scale grid energy storage, and aerospace applications [8–11].

An electrolyte is a critical component, serving as the medium for ion transport between cathode and anode, significantly influencing energy density, safety, and lifespan of batteries [12]. The three main types of battery systems currently under investigation include organic liquid batteries, all-solid-state batteries, and aqueous batteries.

Organic liquid batteries, also known as conventional Li-ion batteries (LIBs), are characterized by their use of liquid electrolytes. These electrolytes typically comprise a Li salt dissolved in organic solvents. The most common solvents include carbonates and ethers. Carbonates such as ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC), and ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC), along with ethers such as 1,2-dimethoxyethane (DME) and 1,3-dioxolane (DOL), are widely used [13,14]. Liquid LIBs encounter numerous challenges and limitations. First, a major safety concern arises from the flammability of organic solvents [15]. Second, these batteries exhibit limited energy density. Commercial carbonate-based electrolytes struggle to operate at voltages above 4.3 V due to oxidative decomposition, causing the formation of gas and solid byproducts [16]. One strategy to address this issue involves the utilization of cathode and anode materials with higher specific capacities, such as layered Li transition metal oxide (LLMO) cathodes [17,18] and Li metal [19] or Si-based [20] anodes. However, Li metal anodes (LMAs) in Li-metal batteries often suffer from dendrite growth [21]. Additionally, carbonate-based electrolytes react strongly with LMAs, leading to the formation of porous solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layers and continuous electrolyte decomposition. Although ether-based electrolytes offer better compatibility with LMAs, they are prone to decomposition at high voltages [22]. Finally, while the cost of LIBs has significantly decreased by 97 % since Sony Corp. launched the first commercial LIB, expenses still pose a major drawback [23].

These drawbacks have intensified the search for new battery technologies. Na-ion batteries (SIBs) offer an economic advantage due to the abundance of Na, its lower cost, and the ability to utilize existing Li-ion manufacturing infrastructure. Conversely, poor Na+ kinetics, unstable SEI layers, high volumetric strain, low electronic conductivity, and limited cycling stability significantly impede their commercial viability [24–26]. Notably, Li-metal batteries possess exceptionally high energy density, with Li–O2 batteries offering a theoretical specific energy of up to 11.4 kWh kg−1, the highest among metal–air systems [27]. However, they are challenged by the formation of Li dendrites, inhomogeneous and unstable SEI layers, volumetric expansion, and issues with Li+ solvation structure [21,28]. Ionic liquid electrolyte systems are used on Mars. Li–CO2 batteries that work well in CO2-rich atmospheres offer high discharge capacities enabled by spinel oxide catalysts [29].

All-solid-state batteries (ASSBs) represent the second type of battery technology. They employ solid-state electrolytes (SSEs) in place of liquid electrolytes. SSEs are broadly classified into polymers and inorganic types. Polymer SSEs include materials such as poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) and composite polymer electrolytes, which combine solid polymers with inorganic electrolytes [30]. Inorganic SSEs are further categorized into oxide-based, sulfide-based, and halide-based materials. Oxide-based SSEs include garnet-type Li7La3Zr2O12 (LLZO), perovskite-type lithium lanthanum titanate (LLTO), and NASICON-type lithium aluminum titanium phosphate (LATP). Sulfide-based SSEs include materials such as glass-ceramic Na3PS4 [31], Li10GeP2S12, Li3PS4, and Li6PS5Cl [32]. Halide-based SSEs include fluorides and chlorides such as Li3MCl6 (where M = Y, In) [30,33–35].

One of the key advantages of SSEs is their improved safety due to non-flammability, which eliminates the risks of leakage and thermal runaway. SSEs offer superior thermal stability and can operate across a wider temperature range (up to 200 °C), which is particularly beneficial for electric vehicles (EVs). ASSBs enable the use of Li metal or anode-free configurations, resulting in significantly higher energy densities. Additionally, SSEs exhibit enhanced electrochemical and mechanical reliability with longer operational lifetimes, making them more resistant to degradation, mechanical shock, and vibrations [35–37].

Despite these advantages, a major limitation of ASSBs is the low ionic conductivity of most solid electrolytes (SEs). Only a few sulfide-based SEs achieve the target conductivity of ∼10−2 S cm−1 [38]. Furthermore, interfacial instability between SEs and electrodes often leads to the formation of SEIs and cathode electrolyte interphases (CEIs), which hinder ion transport and contribute to polarization and self-discharge [37]. Mechanical mismatch between brittle inorganic SEs and electrodes can cause cracking or delamination, particularly under thermal or mechanical stress [39].

The third type of battery is aqueous batteries, which use water as the main electrolyte component. These electrolytes contain Li salts such as Li2SO4, LiNO3, and lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI) [40,41]. Water-based electrolytes exhibit relatively higher ionic conductivity than organic liquid electrolytes [40], but performance and stability-enhancing additives are typically required [42]. Aqueous batteries broadly include aqueous LIBs (ALIBs), aqueous SIBs (ASIBs), and aqueous Zn-ion batteries (AZIBs). Among them, AZIBs are most extensively studied due to the chemical stability of Zn in aqueous environments, ionic radius similar to that of Li, and higher volumetric capacity (5855 mAh cm−3) than Li (2061 mAh cm−3) and Na (1129 mAh cm−3) [43–45].

Although aqueous batteries offer safe, low-cost, non-toxic, and environmentally friendly solutions [46,47], one of their primary limitations is the narrow electrochemical stability window (ESW) of water (∼1.23 V), which restricts their energy density. Beyond this voltage limit, parasitic reactions such as hydrogen and oxygen evolution occur [48]. AZIBs face substantial challenges at both electrodes. Cathodes, particularly those operating via conversion mechanisms, suffer from side reactions that generate inert byproducts and hinder Zn2+ transport. On the anode side, Zn dendrite formation, hydrogen evolution, and dead Zn accumulation lead to short circuits, low coulombic efficiency, and limited cycle life [45,49]. Recent advancements in aqueous metal–air batteries, such as Zn–air and Mg–air systems, highlight the role of catalysts like spinel NiFe2O4 and palladium selenides, which exhibit efficient oxygen reduction and evolution reactions through multi-step pathways and offer promising rechargeability and stability characteristics [50,51].

Key technological milestones for each battery chemistry are summarized in Fig. 1 . This review emphasizes LIBs and SIBs as representative examples of organic liquid batteries. In these systems, research is primarily focused on cathode and anode materials, as these components often limit overall performance. Conversely, research on ASSBs concentrates on SEs, while studies on aqueous batteries focus on developing advanced electrolytes. Beyond the mainstream battery chemistries discussed, recent advances also include photo-assisted batteries [52,53], Martian-atmosphere-compatible systems [54], fast-charging batteries [55], and wide-temperature rechargeable technologies [56,57]. These innovations are important for extreme conditions. Therefore, in each section, some materials specifically designed for these advanced technologies are also discussed.

The advancement of next-generation batteries requires a comprehensive strategy that explores emerging technologies and enhances existing systems. As aforementioned, several critical challenges remain unresolved, including limited energy density, capacity fading, Li dendrite formation, safety concerns, and instability of the SEI layer. Therefore, a deep understanding of the dynamic structural, chemical, and interfacial phenomena in both the bulk and interfacial regions during battery operation is essential.

One critical direction involves elucidating the mechanism underlying charge storage dynamics in various electrode materials during ion insertion. Intercalation-type materials reversibly accommodate ions within the host structure through pre-existing openings or channels (one-dimensional (1D), two-dimensional (2D), or three-dimensional (3D) pathways), inducing minimal structural disruption. Alloying-type materials (such as Si, Ge, and Sn) react directly with Li ions to form alloys, enabling significantly higher Li storage capacities than intercalation reactions, but typically resulting in substantial volume expansion and capacity fading. Conversion-type materials undergo a redox-driven transformation into nanoscale metal clusters embedded in a Li-rich matrix. Although these materials provide much higher capacities compared to intercalation-type reactions, they experience large voltage hysteresis, low-rate performance, and rapid capacity degradation [72–75]. For example, in LiFePO4 (LFP) cathodes, dynamic studies have revealed that particle size, C-rate, and temperature alter the charge storage mechanism in batteries, thereby affecting cycling performance [76–78].

Another important bulk-level consideration is the chemical evolution of electrode materials during cycling. In ASSB, maintaining a homogeneous Li distribution is critical, as heterogeneous diffusion can lead to uneven utilization of active materials and capacity fading [79].

Contrarily, the electrode–electrolyte interface is a primary site for critical electrochemical processes and degradation phenomena. The formation of SEI or CEI, oxygen loss, and Li dendrite growth are directly linked to capacity fading and safety issues. For instance, oxygen loss from layered Li transition metal oxide cathodes contributes to structural degradation [80], gas evolution due to side reactions with electrolytes [81], and defect formation [82]. Understanding these phenomena is essential for identifying the underlying causes of capacity fading and failure. Fig. 2 presents a schematic summarizing the major degradation pathways across various battery components.

Characterization techniques play a critical role in advancing comprehensive knowledge of the dynamic behavior of rechargeable battery materials. These methods reveal complex structural, chemical, and electrochemical processes that directly impact battery performance. This understanding allows for the design of stable and predictable materials, reducing the need for trial-and-error during scale-up. A significant challenge in scaling up production is ensuring the material is free from performance-degrading defects. In situ X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), and Raman can be used during production to monitor when the desired structure appears. By identifying early-stage issues like lithium plating, particle cracking, or phase instability, in situ tools reduce the risk of failure when transitioning from lab to industrial scale. Additionally, some in situ tools are being adapted for real-time process monitoring and quality control, making them increasingly relevant to manufacturing environments.

Characterization techniques in battery research are often categorized based on the source of the probing signal, including electron, neutron, X-ray, and light-based methods. Each technique offers distinct advantages in terms of resolution, scale, capability, and application. Table 1 summarizes several characterization techniques and their key features. In this review, we focus on electron microscopy (EM) and X-ray-based techniques, which are among the most widely adopted methods in battery research. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) offer high spatial resolution, enabling detailed morphological and compositional analysis at the nanoscale. X-ray-based methods provide broader applicability for structural and chemical analyses. For instance, XRD offers insights into crystal structures and phase transitions, while XAS enables elemental analysis of oxidation states and local coordination environments. Furthermore, X-ray tomography and X-ray fluorescence microscopy (XFM) facilitate the 3D imaging and spatial mapping of chemical and structural heterogeneities. Notably, both electron and X-ray-based methods support in situ and operando measurements and can be integrated with complementary techniques to enhance analytical depth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of in situ, operando, and ex situ analyses
ce:section.ce:para: In situ, operando, and ex situ characterization techniques are commonly employed to analyze battery materials under different conditions. Ex-situ characterization examines battery components after they have been removed from the electrochemical cell. However, these techniques do not capture real-time dynamic reaction processes and may misrepresent material behavior due to possible contamination or structural alterations during sample handling. Contrarily, in situ techniques enable the observation of materials within an assembled cell under both static and dynamic conditions. Nevertheless, some in situ configurations may not fully replicate actual operational environments. Operando techniques, by comparison, are specifically designed to monitor battery materials under realistic working conditions, allowing a direct correlation between physicochemical changes and electrochemical performance [124,125].

The primary advantage of in situ and operando techniques over ex situ methods is their ability to continuously monitor electrochemical reactions in real time. This real-time observation significantly enhances data reliability and precision while capturing transient intermediate phases that are generally missed by ex situ techniques. In situ and operando techniques eliminate the risk of contamination or irreversible structural changes introduced during sample preparation. Consequently, these methods offer more accurate insights into reaction mechanisms and critical intermediate phases, such as SEI formation in LIBs. However, in situ and operando analyses typically require more intricate and specialized experimental setups. An additional concept involves synchrotron radiation, a type of high-intensity electromagnetic radiation generated when relativistic charged particles are accelerated along a circular trajectory. Synchrotron-based in situ and operando techniques have become particularly valuable due to their high intensity, greater penetration depth, and superior spatial resolution [126].

In this review, we examine in situ and operando EM and X-ray-based techniques as applied to LIBs and SIBs, ASSBs, and aqueous batteries. These techniques are powerful tools for real-time observation of electrochemical processes. We describe the underlying principles, experimental configurations, and technical challenges associated with each method. We then summarize key findings derived from in situ and operando techniques to elucidate electrochemical phenomena occurring within both bulk materials and interfacial regions. Additionally, we highlight specific parameters that govern failure mechanisms and are critical to the development of next-generation energy storage systems. This review offers technical insights into interpreting characterization results and provides mechanistic insights into the dynamic processes occurring within battery systems. Collectively, these insights aim to assist researchers in selecting the most appropriate characterization methods for investigating targeted electrochemical behaviors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Techniques of operation
X-ray-based techniques
ce:section.ce:para: X-rays are widely employed for non-destructive analysis of rechargeable batteries and their electrochemical behavior. These techniques offer valuable insights into crystal structure, chemical composition, and morphological features across multiple length scales. As X-rays interact with matter based on electron cloud density, their interaction strength depends on the atomic number of the elements. Elements with higher atomic numbers (such as Pb, Au, Fe, and Co) exhibit stronger interactions, while those with lower atomic numbers (such as Al, C, and F) offer significantly weaker associations [127]. These interactions give rise to phenomena such as absorption, scattering, and emission, which are leveraged for both qualitative and quantitative analyses. X-ray techniques are generally categorized into three main modalities: imaging, scattering, and spectroscopy [128]. Each category contributes uniquely to a deeper understanding of battery operation. Although lab-based instruments support a wide range of applications, synchrotron sources offer significantly higher intensity and energy, enabling faster measurement and superior spatial resolution. However, beam-induced damage to battery materials remains a persistent challenge [129]. Commonly employed methods in in situ and operando X-ray-based battery studies include XRD for scattering, XAS and X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) for spectroscopy, and transmission X-ray microscopy (TXM) or scanning X-ray transmission microscopy (STXM) for imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando X-ray diffraction (XRD)
ce:section.ce:para: XRD operates on the principle that X-rays interact with the periodic arrangement of atoms in a crystalline material, resulting in scattering in specific directions. This scattering arises from the interaction of X-rays with the electron clouds surrounding atomic nuclei. Diffraction occurs when the scattered X-rays interfere constructively. This constructive interference is described by Bragg's law: nλ = 2d sin θ, where n represents the order of reflection, λ is the wavelength of the incident X-rays, d is the interplanar spacing, and θ is the angle of incidence [130,131]. The resulting diffraction peaks provide critical information about the crystallographic structure, phase composition, and crystal defects of the material [124,132].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando X-ray spectroscopy (XPS, XAS)
ce:section.ce:para: XAS probes the local electronic and chemical structure of specific elements by scanning the incident X-ray energy across their absorption edge and recording the absorption coefficient μ(E) [133]. XAS is highly sensitive to oxidation states, chemical bonding environments, spin states, and coordination [134,135]. The resulting spectrum is typically divided into two regions: XANES and extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) [136,137].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is a surface-sensitive technique that measures the kinetic energy of photoelectrons emitted from core electronic levels. Both XAS and XPS involve core-level electronic transitions but operate via different mechanisms. In XPS, core electrons are ejected by incident X-rays, and their kinetic energies are measured to determine binding energies. In XAS, core electrons are excited to unoccupied states, and the absorption is monitored as a function of photon energy [133].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando X-ray imaging (XRT, XCT, TXM, STXM, XFM)
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray imaging techniques exploit the interaction between X-rays and matter to visualize structural, chemical, and morphological information in both 2D and 3D. The primary principle involves the attenuation of an X-ray beam, which varies with the electron density of different materials [138,139].

Techniques such as X-ray tomography (XRT) and X-ray computed tomography (XCT) reconstruct 3D images from multiple 2D projections acquired at various angles [140]. XCT enables spatial and temporal quantification of crystallographic structures both within and between particles [141]. Advanced imaging methods, such as TXM and STXM, employ focused X-ray beams to produce high-resolution images, where spatial resolution depends on the beam spot size. TXM typically achieves a resolution of ∼30 nm [142], with synchrotron sources enabling resolutions as fine as 15 nm [143]. STXM provides resolutions in the range of 12–40 nm [144,145]. XFM utilizes a microfocused X-ray beam to raster-scan the sample and generate 2D elemental distribution maps with microscale spatial resolution [146]. Combining these imaging modalities with spectroscopic techniques enables the extraction of complementary chemical information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando X-ray cell configuration
ce:section.ce:para: Designing an experimental setup for in situ and operando X-ray-based techniques requires the integration of electrochemical functionality with X-ray transparency and spatial accessibility. The configuration must enable real-time probing without compromising cell integrity. This necessitates compatibility with various X-ray modes, including transmission, reflection, and tomography, while minimizing background noise.

In 1978, Chianelli et al. developed the first in situ XRD setup for batteries, comprising an Al body with a Be window [147]. The modified coin cell remains a widely used solution for laboratory-scale measurements. In 1997, an in situ XRD cell was designed using standard coin cell hardware (Fig. 3 a). A 1.75 cm hole was punched into the stainless-steel can to allow X-ray penetration, and sealed with a 2.16 cm Be window affixed using pressure-sensitive adhesive. An Al spacer ring was inserted between the cathode and the window to prevent direct contact [148]. Common window materials included Kapton, Be, polyethylene, Mylar, and glassy carbon, selected for their high X-ray transmissivity and chemical stability [149–152].

The intrinsic toxicity of Be limits its industrial applications [153]. To overcome the limitation, pouch-type two-electrode electrochemical cells were custom-designed with square-shaped windows (2 cm × 2 cm), sealed with 200-μm-thick Kapton film to enable X-ray transparency (Fig. 3b). The working electrodes were fabricated by dip-coating Al grid current collectors with an electrode slurry [154]. Kapton was used as the window material for aqueous battery studies due to its chemical stability in water-based environments, unlike Be and Al, which react with water [155]. The AMPIX electrochemical cell was employed due to its compatibility with synchrotron-based scattering and spectroscopic techniques. The cell comprised a chemically resistant, electrically insulating body with a cylindrical cavity housing the battery stack (Fig. 3c). An X-ray-transparent glassy carbon window enabled transmission. The AMPIX design supported a range of X-ray modalities, including XRD, pair distribution function (PDF), small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS), and XAS [156].

Liu et al. [90] introduced the first operando soft XAS (sXAS) with SSEs for LIBs (Fig. 3d). The cell employed a polymer electrolyte comprising PEO and LiTFSI. Al foils were precision-drilled with an array of 50 μm-diameter holes spaced 100 μm apart. Zaghib et al. [157] developed a modified CR2032 coin cell for real-time XANES and synchrotron XRD measurements, aimed at investigating interactions between an uncharged Li2FeSiO4 (LFS) cathode and a LiPF6-based electrolyte (Fig. 3e). The coin cell casing was modified to include Kapton windows. Assembled cells were stored for 96 h prior to electrochemical cycling to promote interfacial interactions, followed by a single charge and discharge cycle at a C/20 rate.

For imaging or tomography techniques such as TXM and XRT, the electrochemical cell must accommodate rotation and minimize X-ray absorption by the casing [158]. The first in situ TXM characterization of electrodeposited Li was achieved using bespoke symmetrical electrochemical coin cells specifically designed to enable uninterrupted 360° X-ray imaging (Fig. 1f) [159]. In this setup, Li metal electrodes were formed around Cu wires and encapsulated within Kapton capillaries with a 1 mm inner diameter. A 2 mm inter-electrode gap filled with 1 M LiPF6 in a 1:1 volumetric mixture of EC and DMC. The entire assembly was sealed with epoxy to prevent leakage and contamination. This configuration is also capable of synchrotron-based imaging.

The commercial application of batteries motivates researchers to investigate their behavior in real-time, including under elevated temperature conditions. To this end, a custom-designed operando XRD cell has been developed to enable analysis at both room and elevated temperatures (Fig. 3g). The cell is constructed with a heated base plate and features a Be X-ray window mounted on a steel sheet using conductive paste to ensure gas-tight sealing and electrical conductivity [160].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando X-ray-based analyses for Li-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, we examine in situ and operando X-ray-based techniques applied to intercalation, alloying, and conversion-type electrode materials in LIBs. We review relevant studies to understand electrode behavior under various operating and synthesis conditions.

As aforementioned, different types of charge storage mechanisms exist. Notably, intercalation-type materials follow either a single-phase (solid solution reaction) or a phase-transition mechanism. In single-phase reactions, XRD patterns exhibit only peak shifts without the emergence of new peaks. In phase-transition reactions, both original and new peaks emerge together, reflecting a two-phase region. Single-phase reactions offer enhanced structural stability, while phase-transition reactions provide higher capacities but may induce structural stress.

Li et al. [161] reported a single-phase reaction in Li-rich layered oxide Li1.2Ni0.2Mn0.6O2. As shown in Fig. 4 a, in situ XRD and refined lattice parameters were analyzed over the first two electrochemical cycles. At the onset of charging, the (003) peak shifted to lower angles, corresponding to an increase in the c-lattice parameter due to Li + extraction from Li layers. This extraction enhanced electrostatic repulsion between O2 slabs, leading to unit cell expansion along the c-axis. When the voltage reached 4.5 V, the (003) peak began to shift back to higher angles as Li+ was removed from the transition metal layers, a process associated with the activation of O2 redox reactions. During discharge, the peak positions reverted, confirming Li + reinsertion and the reduction of transition metals and O2 species. These reversible shifts were also observed in the second cycle, confirming structural reversibility. Additionally, the phase transition dynamics of micron-sized disordered LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4 revealed intermediate states within the solid-solution domain during the two-phase transitions. Operando time-resolved XRD (TR-XRD) identified these intermediate states in addition to the stable Li1, Li1/2, and Li0 phases [162]. These intermediates appeared as weak diffraction features between the dominant peaks in the TR-XRD profiles (Fig. 4b). TEM and electron energy-loss spectroscopy (EELS) measurements were employed to probe the origin of these intermediates. These measurements supported a moving phase boundary model, indicating the coexistence of Li-rich and Li-poor domains within individual particles.

Conversely, the phase-transition reaction mechanism was observed in layered Li2MoO3 cathode materials by in situ XRD. The diffraction patterns exhibited two distinct phases (Fig. 4c) [163]. In the initial stage (0<x < 0.5), peak shifts such as (003), (107), and (108) indicated expansion along both the c and a-axes. This behavior corresponded to a solid solution reaction in Phase I, characterized by decreasing d-spacing. As delithiation proceeded to 0.5<x < 1.0, a new phase (Phase II) emerged and grew while Phase I progressively diminished. Phase II retained the layered structure but exhibited a larger unit cell with expanded lattice parameters in both directions, resulting from Mo migration into the Li layers. A flat voltage plateau in this range indicated a two-phase reaction. When Li extraction exceeded x > 1.0, Phase I vanished completely, and Phase II continued evolving via a solid solution reaction.

Numerous studies have examined the phase transformation mechanisms of LixFePO4 (LxFP) and LiMnyFe1−yPO4 (LMFP) to elucidate their structural evolution during electrochemical cycling. Accordingly, Section 2.3 provides a more detailed discussion of in situ TEM studies on LFP cathode materials, with particular emphasis on EM perspectives.

Typical delithiation and lithiation mechanisms in both LFP and LMFP involve a two-phase reaction, characterized by the coexistence of Li-rich and Li-poor phases during cycling. However, several key parameters, such as particle size [76], C-rate [77], and temperature [78], induce a transition from the two-phase mechanism to a single-phase solid-solution behavior.

In situ synchrotron XRD was performed on LFP cathodes at a high current rate (10C) to elucidate phase transformation mechanisms under non-equilibrium conditions [164]. Fig. 4d(A) presents a color-coded intensity map of selected XRD peaks, (200), (211), (020), and (301), across the first five charge and discharge cycles. The XRD data were aligned alongside the corresponding voltage profile. During cycling, LFP peaks disappeared upon charge (delithiation), while FePO4 (FP) peaks simultaneously emerged. The reverse process occurred during discharge (lithiation). The significant intensity between LFP and FP peaks indicated deviations from equilibrium lattice parameters and confirmed the formation of intermediate phases. Fig. 4d(B) presents representative diffraction patterns at key points during the first two cycles. At the beginning of the cycle, the diffraction peaks were sharp and symmetrical (pattern a). However, significant asymmetric broadening appeared during cycling (patterns d, f, g, l, p). This asymmetry was particularly evident between the LFP and FP reflections. Such asymmetry highlighted compositional heterogeneity and the formation of non-equilibrium solid-solution phases (LixFePO4, 0<x < 1). After cycling, the peaks did not fully return to their original positions or shapes (pattern r), indicating persistent structural distortion and residual compositional inhomogeneity induced by high-rate cycling.

Operando X-ray diffraction computed tomography (XRD-CT) is a powerful technique for directly capturing spatially resolved reaction dynamics within LFP electrodes. Fig. 4e(A) presents the galvanostatic voltage profile (red curve) at a constant current rate (C/10), with blue dots indicating the time points of tomography scans. Grey squares represent the average LFP phase fraction obtained from XRD-CT analysis across the entire electrode. Fig. 4e(B) displays color-coded maps of local Li composition (derived from LFP phase fractions) across different horizontal electrode layers at various stages of cycling. Each layer is separated vertically by 0.1 mm. The map reveals both spatial and temporal heterogeneity of Li distribution across the electrode. Notably, regions near the separator and current collector reach full delithiation more rapidly, as indicated by the lower LFP fractions shown in darker blue. Inner layers remain less delithiated, exhibiting higher LFP fraction (green or yellow), highlighting significant gradients in reaction kinetics within the electrode [165].

Such an inhomogeneous Li distribution has been directly observed at finer spatial resolution using operando TXM-XANES. At the multi-particle level, studies have demonstrated a rate-dependent intercalation pathway. At higher current rates, the phase transformation proceeds inhomogeneously, with particles undergoing sequential conversion to fully delithiated FP. Conversely, lower current rates yield a more concurrent and homogeneous phase distribution across particles [166]. Operando TXM-XANES data of single-crystal LFP delithiation are shown in Fig. 4f. The visualization reveals FP domain growth as blue regions expanding inward from the (100) and (001) surfaces. This observation confirms the presence of 2D Li diffusion in micrometer-scale particles. The study represents the first experimental evidence that Li can be extracted from non-(010) surfaces, thereby challenging the conventional assumption that only (010) surfaces are electrochemically active [169].

In comparison, although LMFP cathode materials share a similar olivine structure with LFP, they exhibit a more complex delithiation and lithiation mechanism that is highly sensitive to the Mn/Fe ratio and the synthesis method. In general, the Fe2+/Fe3+ redox reaction proceeds via a single-phase solid solution, whereas the Mn2+/Mn3+ reaction favors a two-phase transition due to sluggish kinetics and the Jahn–Teller distortion associated with Mn3+. Yang et al. [170] demonstrated that equimolar LiMn0.5Fe0.5PO4 (with a 1:1 Mn/Fe ratio) nanocrystals synthesized via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method underwent a single-phase transition during the entire delithiation process. This behavior was attributed to the homogeneous distribution of Mn and Fe cations, and the stepwise redox reactions of Fe2+/Fe3+ and Mn2+/Mn3+. The behavior was observed exclusively in equimolar compositions, whereas in situ XRD revealed a two-phase transition in LiMn0.7Fe0.3PO4 [171]. However, introducing conductive carbon via Li3V2(PO4)3 doping expanded the single-phase reaction regime in LiMn0.7Fe0.3PO4/C, which further improved electrochemical performance [171]. These findings underscored that achieving a single-phase transition with zero Li miscibility gap in equimolar LiMn0.5Fe0.5PO4 significantly enhanced battery performance. The uniform solid-solution behavior and redox-active Fe were associated with improved electrochemical performance compared to compositions that exhibited two-phase transitions, as the absence of phase separation reduced lattice mismatch and enhanced reaction kinetics [170–174].

Graphite, another important intercalation-type material, has been extensively studied using in situ X-ray-based techniques such as XRD, XPS, and XAS [175,176]. In situ X-ray Raman scattering (XRS) spectroscopy has been employed with a custom-designed pouch cell to enable acquisition of the XRS spectrum while simultaneous XRD measurements confirm phase composition [177]. The results reveal that the peak intensity increases with delithiation and shifts to higher 2θ. Therefore, significant modifications in the electronic structure are required with a decrease in Li content. Furthermore, the sharp and well-defined nature of the peaks indicates that the electrode exists predominantly in a single phase at each state of charge with no significant presence of mixed phases. Mathiesen et al. [178] examined the phase transitions and structural evolution of graphite electrodes during lithiation and delithiation with operando high-resolution powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD). During discharge, a distinct shift of the (00l) peaks toward lower 2θ angles was observed. This shift indicated an expansion of the interlayer distance due to Li intercalation. Additionally, the appearance and evolution of multiple peaks indicated the coexistence of various staging phases, suggesting the presence of both two-phase and solid-solution behaviors. Yao et al. reported high Li concentrations near the electrode surface even during moderate 1C charging, as revealed by operando XRD [179], thereby identifying conditions conducive to Li plating. This finding enables battery designers to modify electrode structures to mitigate Li plating risks. To further explore the factors governing phase evolution, operando XRD studies have revealed that up to four distinct phases can coexist during intercalation and deintercalation at elevated temperatures, in contrast to room temperature. Additionally, higher C-rates are found to suppress the classical staging behavior. These observations collectively suggest that both temperature and cycling rate critically affect phase formation and lattice expansion [160].

Understanding the alloying and de-alloying behavior of Si as a promising anode material candidate is important for optimizing the electrochemical and mechanical performance of electrodes. Misra et al. [180] performed in situ XRD analysis to study the phase transition of crystalline Si. During lithiation, the Si (111) peak fading indicated the amorphization of Si. New peaks emerged corresponding to two unidentified crystalline phases. These phases were inconsistent with known Li–Si or electrolyte-based phases and were attributed as products of Li reacting with the Au catalyst used during Si nanowire growth. Finally, crystalline Li15Si4 formed at low voltages during deep lithiation, as reflected by a voltage plateau. During delithiation, in situ XRD revealed Li15Si4 disappearance without crystalline Si formation, indicating a resulting phase of amorphous LixSi. As the metastable phases turned detrimental in the long cycle life of the battery, synthesis conditions (e.g., temperature) were adjusted, and a lower cutoff voltage was used to avoid their formation.

As a promising conversion-type anode material, Co3O4 has been extensively studied using in situ X-ray-based techniques to monitor its structural and phase evolution. Chang et al. [181] utilized in situ XRD to investigate the phase transformation behavior of Co3O4 nanograin-interconnected secondary particles (NISPs) during lithiation, comparing them with conventional Co3O4 nanoparticles (NPs). During lithiation, the weakening of characteristic Co3O4 peaks such as (111), (311), and (222), indicated progressive amorphization and the formation of LixCo3O4. The final product, metallic Co, was not directly detected by XRD due to its amorphous nature or nanoscale size. Conversely, Co3O4 NPs exhibited a CoO intermediate phase during lithiation. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations supported these observations by showing lower Li diffusion barriers at the surface relative to the bulk, thereby explaining the preferential formation of CoO in surface regions of NPs. The reduced formation of the insulating CoO phase in Co3O4 NISPs enhanced charge transport and improved internal cell resistance. In situ XANES and EXAFS analyses revealed that during charging, A fraction of Co was partially re-oxidized to Co3O4 and CoO [182]. XANES spectra further showed that some Co NPs became electrically isolated, attributed to encapsulation within a polymeric layer or entrapment in a Li2O matrix. This isolation prevented full participation of the particles in the redox reaction. Such incomplete reversibility of the conversion reaction contributed to capacity loss. A similar trend was observed in operando grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction (GIXD) of a mesoporous NiCo2O4 anode. After delithiation, only weak signals from amorphous NiO and Co3O4 were detected, suggesting incomplete structural recovery and mesopore collapse. In contrast, larger-pore mesoporous NiCo2O4 anodes exhibited improved structural reversibility under similar operando GIXD conditions [183]. Therefore, understanding these irreversible structural changes is essential for designing more stable electrode materials [74].

The SEI layer plays a critical role in maintaining battery stability, and its degradation, primarily driven by volume fluctuations, dendrite formation, and electrolyte-induced side reactions, significantly affects overall battery performance. A study used in situ XPS to investigate how electrolyte concentration influenced SEI formation and ion intercalation in LIBs [184]. Measurements were conducted on graphite electrodes using ionic liquid electrolytes (ILEs) under realistic operating conditions. In low-concentration electrolytes, a porous SEI layer was formed with both organic species, such as C–F and C–N, and inorganic species, such as LiF, Li2S, and Li3N. This loosely structured SEI layer failed to prevent co-intercalation of electrolyte ions such as EMIm+ and FSI−, which led to electrode exfoliation. It also enabled irreversible Li charge dynamics. In high-concentration electrolytes, the SEI layer was smooth and compact, enriched in inorganic components formed by the reduction of FSI−. This stabilized SEI layer improved Li+ intercalation and suppressed solvent co-intercalation. Operando XRD analysis was also conducted to compare the structural evolution of the NMC811 cathode in cells using gel polymer electrolyte (GPE) and liquid electrolyte [185]. In the GPE-based cell, the lattice parameter changes were significantly reduced. These results indicate that the phase transitions of the NMC811 cathode were more effectively suppressed, and the structural reversibility was better maintained when GPE was used instead of liquid electrolyte.

Failure mechanisms arose from both mechanical and electrochemical stresses, resulting in crack formation and structural degradation. Understanding the nature of cracks in both their formation and propagation assisted in improving battery performance [186]. Insights gained from operando synchrotron-based XRD have enabled strategies to mitigate battery degradation by optimizing testing conditions. Leach et al. [84] demonstrated that lowering the upper cutoff voltage (e.g., 4.2 V instead of 4.3 V) reduced c-lattice collapse and alleviated stress-induced fatigue in Ni-rich NMC811 cathodes. Although higher voltages initially yielded greater capacity, they accelerated the formation of electrochemically inactive “fatigued” phases and increased long-term degradation. Therefore, using a slightly lower voltage cutoff offered greater balance between energy output and cycle life. Additionally, using moderate C-rates (e.g., 0.5–1C rather than 3C) assisted in maintaining structural integrity. Moreover, incorporating longer constant-voltage (CV) holds at the end of charging cycles (particularly at low current) reduced the kinetic limitations of fatigued regions. Operando XAS analysis further clarified that higher charge rates (4C vs. 1C) may reduce structural distortion and protect the cathode by limiting delithiation-induced volume contraction, suggesting benefits to fast-charging conditions [187]. Additionally, the formation of a cohesive CEI via electrolyte additives such as vinylene carbonate (VC) effectively suppressed surface reconstruction and rock-salt phase formation on Ni-rich NMC cathodes, particularly at ultrahigh voltages (up to 5.2 V). This significantly improved cathode stability and cycling performance [188]. Finally, operando XAS measurements on Li-rich layered oxides confirmed oxygen participation in reversible redox processes alongside transition metals, challenging prior assumptions about irreversible oxygen loss [189]. These findings emphasize the complex interplay of bulk and surface phenomena in cathode degradation and reinforce the importance of targeted electrolyte modifications and optimized cycling parameters to extend battery lifespan.

In situ XCT has provided valuable insight into visualizing electrode degradation mechanisms to understand battery failure. Vanpeene et al. [190] utilized both laboratory and synchrotron X-ray sources to explore the dynamics of microcracks, gas formation, and SEI growth in Si-based electrodes. Their in situ findings revealed that the electrolyte additive fluoroethylene carbonate (FEC) significantly reduced gas formation and electrolyte consumption associated with SEI formation. The SEI layer formed in the presence of FEC was thinner and more uniform, enhancing electrode stability. Complementary to failure analysis, in situ XRD-CT revealed radial gradients within LixMn2O4 particles [141]. Distinct stoichiometric distributions, with Li1.10Mn1.90O4 near the surface and Li1.05Mn1.95O4 at the core, were recorded. Regions experiencing higher phase segregation exhibited higher internal strain, resulting in mechanical issues such as particle cracking. Moreover, knowledge of the spatial distribution of secondary phases, such as Li2MnO3 and LiMnO2, was beneficial for the strategic design of electrodes. For example, the use of stable surface phases mitigated transition metal dissolution, representing a valuable diagnostic tool for the development of next-generation technologies.

Finally, with the widespread commercial use of LIBs, XCT technology has been employed to detect internal structural defects at industrial inspection scales [191,192]. Fig. 4g(b and c) illustrate the deformation of internal electrodes during charge and discharge cycles at different current rates in a small cylindrical cell [167]. Additionally, Fig. 4h(a and c) present the in operando high-frequency tomography results captured 15 s and 1 s before thermal runaway, respectively [168].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando X-ray-based analyses for Na-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: In situ and operando X-ray-based techniques are critical in elucidating sodiation and desodiation in SIBs. Hard carbon (HC) is one of the most promising anode materials for SIB due to its excellent performance and modification capabilities [193].

In situ X-ray techniques have been employed to investigate Na storage mechanisms in HC anodes. Huang et al. [194] studied a N2 and P-co-doped HC (NPHC) with a 3D porous structure using quasi in situ synchrotron XRD coupled with in situ Raman spectroscopy. XRD analysis identified elemental Na formation at low potentials (0.128–0 V), indicating both pore filling and Na+ intercalation. The reported four-stage Na storage mechanism included (I) surface adsorption (OCV–1.0 V), (II) defect adsorption and initial intercalation (1.0–0.128 V), (III) intercalation and nanopore filling (0.128 ∼ −0.0375 V), and (IV) overpotential deposition (−0.0329 V). Similarly, Qiu et al. [195] demonstrated that the sloping region (>0.1 V) in the voltage profile corresponded to Na+ adsorption at defect and surface-active sites, while the flat plateau below 0.1 V arose from Na+ intercalation into graphitic layers. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the diffraction pattern mapping was divided into six segments based on the voltage profile. Segment I corresponded to the sloping voltage region and exhibited no discernible peak shift, indicating Na+ adsorption on defect sites without inducing structural changes. In Segment II, the (002) peak at 26.6° began to split and partially shifted to 26.2°, signifying interlayer expansion due to Na+ intercalation into graphene layers with sufficient spacing. Segment III retained the new peak at 26.2°, confirming continued Na+ insertion into expanded layers similar to Li intercalation in graphite. The sharp decline in reflection intensity during this phase indicated a disordering transition in the carbon structure. During charging (Segments IV to VI), the peaks merged back to their initial positions, confirming the reversibility of Na+ deintercalation and the structural recovery of the carbon framework. These results established that the low-voltage plateau originated from Na+ intercalation into graphitic regions rather than pore filling or surface adsorption. Furthermore, a separate study demonstrated that Ni single atoms enhanced this mechanism by promoting Na+ diffusion and modulating SEI composition, thereby improving ion transport kinetics and structural stability [24]. Collectively, these findings enabled the design of HC anodes with optimized graphitic layer spacing and minimized surface defects to achieve high reversible capacity and improved coulombic efficiency in SIBs.

Further, transition metal sulfides (MSx, M = Fe, Co, Ni, Mn, Cu, etc.) have gained attention as high-capacity anode materials for SIBs owing to their conversion reaction mechanism [197,198]. Understanding their complex Na storage behavior is important for addressing problems such as slow ion diffusion, large volume changes, and low cycle stability. Operando 2D chemical phase mappings of Ni3S2 during the initial discharge and charge processes were captured using TXM (Fig. 5b). During sodiation, a core-shell phase transformation mechanism was observed, where a reaction initiated at the particle edges and gradually moved inward. An interface between the two regions emerged, marking that electrochemical conversion occurred at a rate that outpaced Na + transport. As sodiation continued, cracks began to form due to volume expansion, which led to further sodiation from these cracked surfaces. Thus, the primary particles were pulverized. The cracks also corresponded to the formation of SEI, which consumed Na ions and led to capacity fading. However, during desodiation, the reaction was more homogeneous due to the larger surface area created by the existing cracks. However, the charged particles did not fully recover their initial morphology or chemical state, attributable to the presence of SEI and residual sodium trapped within the structure due to slow diffusion. These operando findings provided crucial insight into the causes of initial irreversibility [196]. Furthermore, the integration of in situ TXM-XANES with operando high-energy XRD (HEXRD) offered complementary insight into the mechanism and guided subsequent material design improvements.

High-entropy oxides (HEOs) are promising candidates for SIBs owing to their high configurational entropy, which stabilizes the O3-type layered structure and enhances cycling stability [199]. In NaCu0.1Ni0.3Fe0.2Mn0.2Ti0.2O2 cathodes, the high-entropy effect enhanced structural stability by delaying the O3 to P3 phase transition [200]. Operando XRD analysis revealed that this transition occurred only after the extraction of 0.32 Na ions per formula unit, which is delayed compared to the 0.2 Na+ threshold in conventional transition metal oxides [201]. This delay facilitated the retention of the O3-type layered structure during electrochemical cycling. Simultaneously, in situ XANES at the K-edges of Cu, Ni, Fe, Mn, and Ti revealed that Mn4+ and Ti4+ were considered to remain electrochemically inactive to maintain the structure, while Fe3+/4+, Cu2+/3+, and Ni2+/4+ actively underwent redox reactions during cycling. These characteristics enabled the material to achieve exceptional reversibility and long-term durability, offering a high specific capacity of 130 mAh g−1 and retaining 87.2 % of its capacity after 100 charge and discharge cycles.

For practical applications of SIBs, cathode materials should deliver high capacity retention under high-rate and high-loading conditions. A novel gradient layer-tunnel composite cathode material, Na0.6Mn0.95Ti0.05O2, was developed to address existing challenges [202]. The material featured a Mn-rich P2 layered bulk that offered high capacity and a Ti-rich tunnel-type outer shell that enhanced structural integrity. In situ XRD and XAS revealed that enhanced electrochemical performance was achieved by multiple synergistic factors. (I) The layered P2 structure showed reversible solid−solution behavior with suppressed irreversible P2-O2 phase change. (II) Minimal changes in Ti–O and Ti–TM bond lengths were observed during cycling. (III) XAS analysis at Ti and Mn K-edges showed that Mn acted as the active redox center with a reversible Mn3+/Mn4+ couple, while Ti4+ remained electrochemically inert and preserved the integrity of the tunnel framework. These observations proved that the gradient composition not only enhanced Na + storage kinetics and structural change reversibility but also retained electrode integrity over prolonged cycling, thereby emphasizing the potential for practical SIB applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando X-ray-based analyses for all-solid-state batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Numerous studies have used X-ray techniques to directly observe changes in the interfaces and materials in ASSBs. These studies improve our understanding of degradation mechanisms, Li transport heterogeneity, and interfacial stability across a range of materials and cell architectures.

Operando synchrotron X-ray computed microtomography has been used to investigate the dynamic evolution of Li and SSE interfaces in Li/Li10SnP2S12/Li symmetric cells during cycling (Fig. 6 a and b) [203]. Void formation during Li stripping was directly visualized and quantified as shown in Fig. 6a(i) and Fig. 6b(b). The loss of contact at the Li/SSE interface was identified as the main cause of cell failure due to local current constriction. At the cathodic interface, interphases grew continuously without Li metal deposition. Three modes of void evolution were observed, including formation during stripping, closure during reduction, and formation at the cathodic interface (Fig. 6b(b–d)). Notably, void formation was independent of interfacial roughness. Contact area maps revealed that interfacial contact loss, particularly at the anodic interface, was correlated to increased overpotential and cell failure. Fig. 6b(e) presents the volume displacement of SSE and electrodes, attributed to the partial molar volume mismatch between Li metal and the interphase, causing overall cell shrinkage and minimizing stack pressure during cycling.

Li heterogeneity and uneven electrochemical reactions within composite electrodes significantly impact the power and energy densities of SSBs. Kimura et al. [79] used operando 3D CT-XANES imaging to monitor Li distribution and reaction dynamics within composite electrodes (Fig. 6c). Their findings demonstrated that active material (AM) loading significantly affected electrochemical uniformity. The electrodes with higher AM content exhibited underutilized regions, particularly within the cores of AM aggregates. These regions were less accessible to Li ions due to limited direct contact with SE particles, which restricted ion transport primarily to intergranular paths through AMs. Therefore, full utilization of the active materials was hindered, leading to lower capacity. Conversely, lower AM loading reduced aggregation, improved connectivity, and enabled uniform reaction distribution throughout the electrode. Complementary findings revealed that Li heterogeneity developed during charging in SSB composite electrodes and did not fully relax, even after long open-circuit holding times (Fig. 6d) [204]. This persistent heterogeneity was marked by the coexistence of highly and poorly charged regions, attributed to elevated interfacial resistance between AM particles and solid electrolyte particles. The resistance was further exacerbated by structural factors such as non-uniform particle orientations, closed pores, and stress-induced chemical potential gradients. This behavior differed significantly from that in liquid-based LIBs, where relaxation tended to be more homogeneous due to improved ionic pathways and fewer mechanical constraints. Collectively, these studies highlight that CT-XANES enables direct 3D visualization of Li dynamics and electrochemical reactions in real-time. Optimal design strategies for SSBs electrodes have been suggested, including mitigating AM aggregation, achieving homogeneous dispersion of AM particles, and the use of deformable AMs that can establish larger interfacial contact areas [204].

Further, operando XRD has been used to analyze the distinct behavior of single-crystal NMC811 (S-NMC) in LIBs and ASSBs [205]. In LIBs, the (003) diffraction peak of S-NMC follows a typical shift pattern during delithiation, initially moving to lower angles and then sharply shifting to higher angles above 4.15 V, indicating c-axis contraction. In ASSBs, although similar peak shifts are observed, the (003) peak at the end of the first charge exhibits significant splitting into three components. These phases are assigned to active, intermediate, and sluggish phases. The sluggish phase is characterized by residual Li content and a lower state of charge, although it normally appears only after extensive cycling in LIBs [206]. An LiNbO3 (LNO) coating demonstrates that the (003) peak shifts normally during cycling without splitting. The findings confirm that the sluggish phase does not form, and its absence assists in minimizing side reactions and enhancing long-term performance in ASSBs. Zhou et al. [207] applied a coating of two conductive polymers (polypyrrole (PPy) and polythiophene (PEDOT)) on a Ni-rich NMC811. These conductive polymer coatings were introduced to stabilize the CEI and suppress lattice oxygen evolution during cycling. In situ XRD revealed improvements in structural stability for NCM@PPy, with the smallest lattice variation (Δc = 0.7711), in comparison to NCM@PEDOT (Δc = 0.7961) and pure NCM811 (Δc = 0.8421)

The shifting of peaks in in situ XRD provides insights into the behavior of different SSEs. Fig. 6e presents the corresponding in situ XRD patterns, focusing on the evolution of the (003) peak during the first cycle for polycrystalline NCA electrodes paired with halide Li3YCl6 (P/LYC sample) and sulfide Li6PS5Cl0.5Br0.5 (P/LPSX sample) solid electrolytes. In P/LYC, the (003) peak shifted significantly during charge, indicating more extensive Li+ deintercalation (higher state-of-charge (SOC)). Conversely, the peak shift in P/LPSX was smaller, suggesting delayed Li+ extraction due to interfacial side reactions [208]. Zhou et al. [209] reported that the sulfide electrolyte glass ceramic Li2S–P2S5 (LPS) with the molar ratio of 70:30 exhibited no peak shift in the in situ XRD patterns. Thus, the bulk crystal structure of LPS remained stable during redox cycling. Such structural preservation contributed to the long-term performance of the LPS-based ASSBs. Moreover, both ex situ and operando XRD indirectly assessed the SOC in ASSBs, offering an alternative to conventional electrochemical tests. This approach mitigated the limitations associated with electrochemical methods, which were influenced by inactive materials and side reactions. Data obtained from liquid electrolyte cells were used for comparison, serving as a reference standard [210].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando X-ray-based analyses for aqueous batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Recent advances in in situ and operando X-ray-based techniques have significantly deepened our understanding of critical phenomena in aqueous battery systems, particularly the mechanisms underlying dendrite formation, growth, and suppression, along with structural evolutions at the electrode–electrolyte interfaces.

Initial aqueous system studies have focused mainly on ALIBs. Phase change offers a deeper understanding of the battery anode interface failure mechanism. Zaghib et al. [155] explored the structure evolution of olivine FePO4 anode and LiMn2O4 cathode during electrochemical cycling in ALIBs. Kapton film was utilized as an X-ray transparent window, enabling real-time monitoring despite the challenges posed by aqueous environments. In situ XRD revealed that olivine FePO4 underwent a reversible two-phase reaction between FePO4 and LiFePO4 during lithiation and delithiation (Fig. 7 a), which was consistent with its known behavior in organic electrolytes and confirmed the intercalation mechanism in aqueous systems. Accordingly, the LiMn2O4 cathode exhibited continuous and reversible lattice expansion and contraction, indicating structural stability throughout cycling. Amorphous FePO4·2H2O transformed nanocrystalline Li0.5FePO4·2H2O during lithiation. In situ XRD, combined with scanning transmission electron microscope (STEM) and EELS, confirmed the Li intercalation mechanisms. Olivine FePO4 offered a higher capacity (163 mAh g−1) and a capacity retention of 70 % at a high rate (20C), making it suitable for power-demanding applications. Conversely, amorphous FePO4·2H2O provided a longer cycle life (92 % capacity retention after 500 cycles), making it ideal for stable, long-term applications. These findings ultimately guided the development of safer and longer-lasting ALIBs.

Aqueous Zn batteries are emerging as promising next-generation energy storage technologies. Understanding Zn plating/stripping and dendrite formation is crucial for improving the reversibility, safety, and longevity of aqueous Zn batteries, as uncontrolled dendrite growth can cause capacity fade, short-circuit, and eventual cell failure. Dendrite growth, dissolution, and regrowth are depicted in Fig. 7b, obtained using operando X-ray radiography [211]. Initially, dendrites were formed at localized surface inhomogeneities after ∼100 s, growing progressively over time. The growth was characterized by primary, secondary, and tertiary branches. During the dissolution step, a positive current led to thinning and eventual collapse of the dendrites, starting from the top and propagating downward. In the regrowth phase (Fig. 7b (A)(K-O)), new dendrites grew from the receded tip and reconnected with the collapsed remnants of the previous cycle. This process formed a denser and more entangled network of dendrites. Regrowth exhibited a "memory effect," where the morphology was strongly influenced by residual structures from the first cycle. These results highlighted the irreversible and progressively denser nature of dendritic structures over charge–discharge cycles. Operando X-ray radiography and 3D SXCT were used to investigate how the presence of a separator influenced battery behavior (Fig. 7b (B)). A microporous separator significantly altered the behavior of Zn dendrites by impeding the formation of new dendrites but not preventing the continued growth of existing ones. Dendrite growth was first observed inside the separator pores after ∼36 s, eventually penetrating through and continuing to grow on top of the separator surface. Unlike the case without a separator, dendrites grew both vertically and laterally [211]. Mass transport limitations inside the separator affected growth rates and morphology.

Further, Qian et al. [212] employed in situ X-ray tomography, operando radiography, and ex situ sXAS to investigate how cycling protocols, substrate morphology, surface chemistry, and substrate curvature modulated Zn plating. Fig. 7c illustrates how surface corrosion and chemical modification of the Cu substrate influenced Zn plating behavior. The results revealed that Zn plated on a fresh Cu substrate formed a uniform and continuous layer over time. When a Cu substrate was pre-soaked in ZnSO4 electrolyte for 48 h, Zn grew non-uniformly. The deposits on the pre-soaked substrate appeared sparse and disconnected at the early stage. These findings, obtained using X-ray imaging as a valuable alternative to EM, suggested the development of dendrite-free and highly reversible aqueous Zn batteries.

Excluding the parameters aforementioned, the mechanism of Mn2+ dissolution–deposition can limit the practical application of aqueous Zn batteries that use manganese oxide cathodes. The technique is particularly important when designing batteries that are low-cost, safe, and environmentally friendly. Wu et al. [213] used operando X-ray fluorescence microprobe (XFM) to gain insight into Mn dissolution in the Zn/α-MnO2 battery system. As shown in Fig. 7d, Mn dissolved into the electrolyte during discharge and redeposited onto the cathode during charge, confirming a reversible dissolution–deposition mechanism. Mn dissolution could be mitigated by controlling Mn2+ solubility in the electrolyte through pH modulation or by changing the electrolyte composition. Another approach concerned electrolyte additives. Introducing Mn2+ to MnSO4 improved capacity retention in Zn/MnO2 batteries [214], Wu et al. revealed that this effect was primarily attributed to the introduction of additional AM rather than suppression of Mn dissolution, forming a part of the dominant reversible charge storage mechanism. In situ SAXRD analysis further revealed the formation of a layered Zn-buserite phase during Zn2+ insertion into α-MnO2, with a new diffraction peak at 8.7°, corresponding to an expanded interlayer spacing of ∼11 Å due to intercalated water molecules [215]. This structural transformation enhances Zn2+ insertion/extraction kinetics by providing additional diffusion channels and acting as a “lubricant” within the MnO2 framework.

Oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) are key degradation mechanisms in aqueous batteries. To investigate these effects, Scharf et al. utilized in situ MicroCT techniques during long-term cycling (>250 cycles) and quantified the high sensitivity of water-based systems to interactions between the electrolyte and current collector materials [216].

The main aim of in situ operando X-ray diffraction, spectroscopy, and imaging techniques is observing structural and chemical changes. In LIBs and SIBs batteries, these techniques have been used to trace phase transformations, heterogeneity of ion diffusion, SEI formation, gas evolution, and mechanical degradation. In ASSBs, these techniques offer insights into interfacial evolution and Li distribution, along with phase reactions inside SSEs. For aqueous systems, X-ray techniques have demonstrated that side reactions such as OER and HER are highly dependent on electrolyte composition. These observations help enhance performance and mitigate failure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges of in situ and operando X-ray-based techniques
ce:section.ce:para:
The most important issues posed by in situ and operando X-ray-based techniques are summarized as follows:
1.
Signal Interference from Non-Target Components: In situ XRD can be affected by signals from other components, such as electrolytes, cell cases, and current collectors, which reduce data clarity. Using thinner collectors and optimized cell designs helps minimize such interference.
2.
Limited Capability for Amorphous and Nanoscale Materials: In situ XRD techniques are less useful for materials lacking long-range order. Such materials result in broad diffraction peaks. Researchers typically use techniques such as pair distribution function (PDF) analysis to examine short-range orders.
3.
Accessibility of Synchrotron-Based Facilities: Synchrotron X-ray techniques provide excellent structural analysis tools. Additionally, 3D visualization techniques such as X-ray CT are performed with synchrotron radiation. However, the high cost of infrastructure makes it hard for several researchers to access the necessary resources.
4.
Challenges in Capturing Fast Charge/Discharge Dynamics: Conventional in situ XRD or XAS methods face challenges in capturing high-rate cycling processes. However, employing time-resolved modes or synchrotron-based instruments improves acquisition time and data collection for researchers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron microscopy (EM) techniques
ce:section.ce:para: In situ and operando EM offers high-resolution imaging to track nanoscale morphological changes in battery electrodes. Techniques such as in situ SEM and TEM provide insights into detailed structure changes and evolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando scanning electron microscopy (SEM)
ce:section.ce:para: SEM is a powerful imaging method for studying surface structure and composition. The technique uses an electron beam from a thermionic or field-emission source with energy levels between 0.5 and 30 KeV. The electromagnetic lenses focus the beam, which is then scanned across the surface [217–219]. In SEM, image formation depends upon signals originating from interactions between the beam of electrons and the specimen. These interactions are categorized as elastic and inelastic interactions [220–222]. SEM can be integrated with other techniques, such as energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) for elemental analysis and electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) for crystallographic information.

Electron microscopes require high vacuum to ensure electron source stability and reduce noise from background scattering. Vacuum conditions required for battery samples are met by using polymer [223,224], solid-state [225], or ILEs [226,227] with low vapor pressure. The quality and contrast of SEM images depend on multiple factors, including atomic number and surface conductivity. Some materials including polymers and Li metal are damaged by prolonged exposure to electron beams. Therefore, imaging conditions are essential for safe operations. Sensitive materials require controlling the temperature by heating or cooling to avoid degradation or evaporation [228,229]. Despite these issues, SEM is critical in battery research, particularly for ASSBs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando SEM cell configuration
ce:section.ce:para: SEM visualization can be categorized into two methods. The first method involves using transfer chambers to move disassembled electrodes, ensuring no environmental contamination. However, this method is generally considered an ex situ technique. The second method utilizes solid electrolytes or low vapor pressure electrolytes, such as ionic liquids, which retain stability under the high vacuum conditions of SEM. In 1988, Baudry et al. [230] reported the morphological evolution of FeS, V6O13, and TiS2 in polymeric electrolyte (polyether with an ethylene oxide base and LiClO4 salt). During transfer into the microscope chamber, the sample encountered the atmosphere for ∼30 s. The first interface investigation was conducted by Orsini et al. using in situ SEM with a 1 M LiPF6 liquid electrolyte [231]. They used a specialized SEM transfer system to prevent air exposure of Li-containing samples. The system included a movable airlock, allowing sample transfer from the glove box to the microscope antechamber without oxidation. The samples were cooled to minimize solvent evaporation in the vacuum chamber of the SEM. The first operando SEM on LIBs was conducted by Raimann et al. [232] using operando environmental SEM (ESEM). Unlike conventional SEM, ESEM allows imaging under low vacuum conditions (<10 Torr) and enables the analysis of wet samples such as electrodes immersed in electrolytes. A custom-designed cell was developed to incorporate a two-electrode configuration with a Li counter electrode (Fig. 8 a). The electrolyte contained high-boiling-point carbonate solvents such as ethylene carbonate and propylene carbonate. This setup enabled real-time tracking of lithiation-induced morphological changes in Sn-based electrodes and revealed crack formation along with particle fracturing due to non-uniform volume expansion.

Rong et al. [233] introduced a liquid-phase electrochemical scanning electron microscopy (EC-SEM) method to visualize Li dendrite growth and dissolution in real-time (Fig. 8b). The experimental setup includes a custom-designed EC-SEM liquid cell made of two microfabricated chips: a top silicon chip with a silicon nitride (SiNx) membrane and a bottom quartz chip with two injection orifices. The electrolyte for Li plating and stripping experiments was 1.0 M LiTFSI in a 1:1 vol mixture of DOL and DME.

In situ focused ion beam SEM (FIB-SEM) can also be used to investigate microstructural evolution [234].
