Fig. 8c shows an experimental setup with a micrometer-sized Sn particle affixed to a W probe via ion-beam C deposition, serving as the positive electrode. Li metal, placed on an SEM stub, served as the negative electrode. A drop of ILE, comprising LiTFSI dissolved in 1-butyl-1-methylpyrrolidinium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl) imide (P14TFSI), was deposited over the Li metal. The negligible vapor pressure of P14TFSI maintained electrolyte stability under the vacuum conditions of the FIB-SEM. Ultimately, a microscale battery configuration was developed to enable in situ EM of single LIBs cathode particles during electrochemical cycling (Fig. 8d). A single Li(Ni0.8Co0.15Al0.05)O2 cathode particle was attached to a nanomanipulator probe using in situ deposition within the FIB-SEM. Once attached, the particle was positioned and immersed in an electrolyte droplet covering a Li4Ti5O12-based anode. The electrolyte, P13 TFSI +0.5 mol kg−1 LiTFSI, was selected for its low vapor pressure to ensure stability in the vacuum environment [235].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando SEM analysis for Li-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Real-time observation of structural, phase, and chemical composition changes offers substantial insight into LIBs with the help of in situ and operando SEM.

While Miller et al. [235] observed grain boundary separation within individual Li(Ni0.8Co0.15Al0.05)O2-based cathode particles, Song et al. [236] used 3D FIB-SEM tomography to depict the propagating cracking and voiding in the electrode architecture over multiple cycles. Fig. 9 a shows cross-sectional SEM images of a Li-rich NMC cathode at different cycling stages: fresh, after the 1st cycle, after the 15th cycle, and after the 50th cycle. These images revealed more structural damage as the number of cycles increased. The particles in the fresh state remained intact and well-defined. After the 1st and 15th cycles, internal voids and cracks were observed. By the 50th cycle, the damage worsened, showing clear fragmentation, voiding, and surface flaking. This visual sequence underscored how repeated lithiation and delithiation caused mechanical degradation and structural breakdown in the cathode.

In situ SEM is generally employed to study the anode-side reactions because it undergoes significant size and morphological changes during charging and discharging cycles [237]. The microstructural evolution and mechanical behavior of Si and SiO particles have been studied. Nano-sized Si particles (∼100 nm) expand by ∼85 % after two cycles without cracking. However, electrochemical sintering and aggregation create rigid electrode structures that crack and cause capacity loss and failure. Conversely, micron-sized SiO particles cycle steadily and maintain their shape when discharged only to 0.1 V (Li12Si7 composition). However, discharging deeper to 15 mV (Li22Si5) causes cracking and pulverization, especially in particles larger than 10 μm. A study reported that the small Si particles prevented fractures but made the electrode rigid, while SiO particles resisted damage with limited cycling but failed under more intense conditions [238].

Accordingly, Chen et al. [239] studied the behavior of Si anodes in a full-cell setup with ILE. They found that Si microparticles experienced non-uniform lithiation, which caused particle fracture, loss of electrical contact, and eventually large irreversible capacity. Conversely, aggregated Si nanoparticles exhibited more isotropic expansion but suffered from the insufficient strength of the interfacial bonds upon cycling. As shown in Fig. 9b–Si thin flakes demonstrated good performance owing to their flexibility, promoting uniform Li diffusion. The flakes demonstrated dual-mode expansion (flat-plate and ribbon types) with minimal cracking. These results underscore the role of particle size and shape in reducing mechanical damage and improving battery performance.

Zhou et al. [234] investigated Sn microparticles using in situ FIB-SEM, uncovering crack initiation during lithiation and the evolution of porosity during delithiation. Fig. 9c presents the microstructural evolution and volume changes of a single Sn particle during its first lithiation and delithiation cycle at a constant current of 0.3 nA. In Fig. 9c(a), the voltage profile and relative volume are shown. The volume of the particle increased gradually during lithiation and reached 384 % at full lithiation. During delithiation, the volume decreased to 204 % by the end of the cycle. Fig. 9c(b–g) present SEM images captured at different stages of lithiation. Cracks began to appear at ∼20 % lithiation and continued to grow as the lithiation progressed. These cracks indicated mechanical stress caused by volume expansion. Fig. 9c(h–k) present SEM images captured during delithiation. As Li was extracted, particles contracted, and nanometer-sized pores were formed on the surface. These pores were mainly found near the edges of the particle and grew more visible toward the end of delithiation. The formation of cracks and pores was linked to Li diffusion and the structural instability of Sn particles. Additionally, the behavior of micron-sized Ge particles has been reported to differ from that of Ge nanoparticles and nanowires [240].

Contrary to alloying materials, the intercalation behavior of V2O5 nanobelts was investigated using a LiCoO2 counter electrode and an ILE [241]. Reversible topochemical transformations were analyzed without significant amorphization or fracturing. Only moderate expansion was detected. Fig. 9d presents real-time morphological changes during lithiation by in situ SEM. Upon applying a negative potential, thickness expansion occurred due to the intercalation of Li. An expansion front moved along the nanobelt. Initially, the front advanced faster than the electrolyte. Further, it was overtaken by the electrolyte front, which migrated through electrocapillary action. As the electrolyte spread, it obscured the expansion front and caused the formation of SEI on the surface. When the bias was reversed to a positive potential, the electrolyte front receded and exposed the remaining SEI bulge. These results suggested the reversibility of Li intercalation and electrolyte movement. Conversely, SEI formation was found to be irreversible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando SEM analysis for Na-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Li et al. [242] investigated the electrochemical plating/stripping dynamics of Na metal on carbon nanofiber (CNF) current collectors. A nanobattery experimental setup was constructed inside the SEM by contacting CNFs, which served as current collectors, with a Na metal counter electrode covered by a naturally grown Na2O solid electrolyte layer. Fig. 10 a (A and B) illustrates the in situ SEM observation of Na plating/stripping at the interface between CNFs and the Na2O electrolyte. Fig. 10a(A)(a–d) present the nucleation and growth of spherical Na particles. Particle expansion and the formation of new particles beneath existing ones were observed. Fig. 10a(A)(e–g) illustrate reversible Na deposition and dissolution during plating/stripping. The process confirmed effective Na transport and highlighted dynamic deposition behaviors. Fig. 10a(B) presents real-time SEM observations of Na transport and deposition along individual CNFs and their interconnected networks. Fig. 10a(B)(a–d) illustrate Na insertion, simultaneous occurrence along two parallel fibers, highlighting the capability for inter-fiber ion transport. Fig. 10a(B)(e–g) illustrate the formation of Na particles distributed uniformly along a single fiber, which subsequently dissolves upon reversing the applied voltage. Fig. 10a(B)(h–j) demonstrate Na deposition across two intersecting fibers, further confirming interconnected Na transport within the CNF network. Additionally, defect-rich areas and mesopores on CNF surfaces were identified as preferential nucleation sites, resulting in localized and repeatable Na deposition. These observations provided critical mechanistic insights into Na nucleation behavior, ion transport pathways, and deposition processes. The findings affirmed CNFs as a highly promising candidate for fabricating dendrite-free Na metal anodes in advanced high-capacity battery systems.

Red phosphorus (RP) has attracted considerable attention as a promising candidate material for SIBs owing to its high theoretical capacity and low cost. However, RP suffers from significant volume changes during cycling [243–245]. Xiang et al. [246] reported that a specifically engineered hierarchical micro/nano-structured Sb-doped RP/carbon composite significantly mitigated the severe mechanical stress (Fig. 10b(a)). In situ SEM demonstrated limited volume expansion (∼156 %) during partial sodiation, significantly lower than the theoretical maximum of ∼499 % for pure RP (Fig. 10b(f–g)). Notably, the particles returned to their original shape during desodiation, suggesting reversible volume change and mechanical integrity (Fig. 10b(h–i)).

Similarly, Sn serves as another promising anode material for SIBs, but it shares the same drawbacks as other alloy-type electrode materials [247,248]. Through in situ SEM characterization, microsized Sn particles were analyzed as they underwent pulverization into NPs, accompanied by the formation of multiple voids and pores during the initial charge–discharge cycle. Real-time imaging explicitly revealed severe particle cracking at both the surface and interior regions, accompanied by substantial structural expansion (∼30–40 %). The findings emphasized the intense mechanical stresses arising from volumetric changes. The in situ SEM results further demonstrated that electrolyte composition strongly influenced parasitic side reactions. These reactions affected the reversibility of the sodiation and desodiation processes. Ether-based electrolytes formed SEI layers predominantly comprising inorganic species with superior mechanical strength. Consequently, higher specific capacities and improved cycling stability were achieved compared to carbonate-based electrolytes [249]. Finally, operando SEM combined with EDS provided real-time insights into Na transport and dynamic distribution within electrode layers. Hiraoka et al. [250] explained that this coupled technique quantitatively estimated the SOC by directly correlating measured changes in Na concentration at the micrometer scale within electrode layers to electrochemical charge–discharge states.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando SEM analysis for all-solid-state batteries
ce:section.ce:para: In situ SEM is a powerful analytical technique that provides insight into interfaces within the ASSBs, such as Li plating, dendrite growth, and SEI formation [113,251–253]. These insights assist researchers in elucidating failure mechanisms and understanding methods to alleviate them [254]. Significant efforts have been dedicated to visualizing Li deposition and stripping. The behavior of Li deposition on the current collector at the anode side poses a significant impact on the performance of an anode-free solid-state battery (AFSSB) [255–258].

Cui et al. [259] studied dynamic Li plating behaviors on ten different metallic substrates in ASSBs. By coupling SEM with an electrochemical workstation, real-time Li deposition was tracked on Cu, Ti, Ni, Cr, Bi, In, Ag, Au, Pd, and Al substrates using a Li1.5Al0.5Ge1.5P3O12 (LAGP) solid-state electrolyte. Fig. 11 a shows the difference in Li growth on Cu and In substrates using time-resolved in situ SEM. On Cu, Li grew as dendrites in an out-of-plane direction with rapid and uneven growth. The growth rate ranged from 55.5 to 106.3 nm s−1 and exhibited strong anisotropy with an anisotropic ratio (AR) of ∼59. On In, Li grew slowly as uniform particles in an in-plane direction. The growth rate was ∼2–3 nm s−1 with isotropic morphology and an AR close to 1. These results highlighted how substrate selection affected the stability and direction of Li deposition. The investigation of the initial Li alloying process on Au and In using in situ SEM revealed that on Au, alloying began at the electrode edge and moved inward due to fast electron flow and concentrated Li+ flux at the edges. The average alloying rate was 2.57 × 102 μm2 s−1. On In, alloying began at the probe tip and spread outward owing to slow electron transport and a lower work function. The alloying rate of In was faster, measured at 2.57 × 103 μm2 s−1. These findings revealed that the conductivity and work function of the substrate influenced how and where Li alloying began.

Further, a series of groundbreaking investigations visualized the formation of Li whiskers, Li dendrites, and intragranular penetration (Fig. 11b(A)), revealing how surface defects, grain boundaries (preferential nucleation shown in Fig. 11b(B)), and local microstructure governed nucleation behavior and morphological instability [260]. These results highlighted the need for microstructural optimization of SSEs to suppress undesirable Li growth and improve battery reliability. Furthermore, using a quantitative in situ SEM approach, a study identified extremely high local-current densities (∼200 mA cm−2) at the roots of vertically growing whiskers, which occurred without immediate Li penetration. Simultaneously, the study revealed the reversible nature of Li penetration, offering a deeper understanding of the “dynamic short-circuiting” mechanism previously proposed [261]. Fig. 11a(C) illustrates the macroscopic outcomes (e.g., penetration, whiskers, uniform plating) of Li deposition under different interface conditions, revealing that the initial interface (bare, alloyed, or lithium-coated) critically influenced Li growth, whether uniform or penetrated into the electrolyte. Additionally, in situ SEM confirmed that Li grew on crystalline electrolytes such as LLZO through stress-driven whisker formation at surface defects, unlike the diffusion-limited dendrite growth observed in liquid systems or glass ceramics [262,263]. This localized, high-rate growth underscored the key role of SSE microstructures in controlling Li deposition and preventing penetration in ASSBs.

Similar to a previous report, Golozar et al. [264] reported that dendrite formation initiated at the anode edge during the early stages of cycling. The formation was driven by an unstable Li/solid polymer electrolyte (Li/SPE) interface before full SEI formation. High activity at grain boundaries caused the formation of isolated Li-rich isles, which were surrounded by decomposition products of the LiTFSI salt, indicating salt reduction and SEI breakdown. As cycling progressed and the rate increased, further dendrite growth, grain boundary opening, and the emergence of Li-depleted regions were detected. The formation of a second anode edge was observed, caused by Li consumption in dendrite growth and "dead Li" accumulation. Additionally, SPE bridges filled voids caused by Li depletion, which led to uneven current distribution, accelerating degradation, and reduced cycle life [265].

The origin and stability of a electrolyte play a crucial role in high-temperature battery performance. Zaghib et al. [266] used in situ SEM-EBSD to examine the high-temperature behavior of a Li–10 wt% Mg alloy anode in ASSBs. When heating beyond the melting point (∼185 °C) of the alloy, the bulk alloy melted, while the surface passivation layer remained solid due to the high melting points of its oxide components. This led to the formation of grain-boundary cracking on the surface. These findings demonstrated the influence of thermal treatment on passivation behavior and contributed to improved safety and performance.

In polyether-based systems with NMC cathodes, failure was caused by the chemical degradation of SPE, which progressively thinned and led to gas evolution and dendrite formation [267]. In Li–sulfur cells, polysulfide dissolution into the SPE created a catholyte and caused an anode passivation layer, resulting in AM loss and capacity fade. A reduction in SPE thickness was observed, which weakened mechanical stability [268]. Yadav et al. [269] investigated sulfide-based SEs using in situ SEM to study failure mechanisms. Two materials were examined: β-Li3PS4 and Li6PS5Cl. In β-Li3PS4, electrical failure occurred due to rapid crack propagation and short circuit formation. Conversely, Li6PS5Cl exhibited mechanical failure caused by delamination of the cathode. In both materials, cracks were observed to form before dendrite growth. This indicated that mechanical stress initiated the failure process. All these phenomena assisted in recognizing the failure mechanism in ASSBs.

In addition to EM, in situ optical microscopy (OM) has also been widely used to visualize Li dendrite formation in real time. Liu et al. [270] demonstrated that in SPE-based lithium metal batteries, Li dendrites exhibit memoryless growth behavior, delaying short circuiting. They showed that this behavior, alongside SEI evolution, can be directly visualized under OM due to the elastic nature of SPE. Complementing this, recent advances reviewed by Li and Wang have highlighted optical methods such as reflection microscopy and dark-field imaging for studying lithium, zinc, and graphite anodes. These methods enable non-destructive visualization of dendrite evolution and SEI formation. Notably, techniques like 3D focus-variation imaging and super-resolution dark-field microscopy have been developed to map morphological changes under different current densities or mechanical constraints. Although these techniques have lower spatial resolution, they offer non-destructive, high-speed imaging and are well-suited for operando studies [271,272].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando SEM analysis for aqueous batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Several studies have focused on improving reversibility in AZIBs. However, uneven Zn plating leads to dendrite growth, which can diminish reversibility and cause short circuits [273–275]. Guo et al. [276] fabricated a ZnSiO3 (ZSO) nanosheet interface layer following an “etching–nucleation–growth” process. This layer helped achieve uniform Zn plating by balancing Zn2+ flux and electric field distribution. They used identical-location SEM (IL-SEM) to track the real-time growth of the ZSO layer. The nanosheets were ultra-thin and densely packed, which suppressed dendrite formation and reduced side reactions such as hydrogen evolution. The layer limited the formation of Zn4SO4(OH)6·xH2O on the anode surface. Consequently, Zn plating and stripping could be reversed. The ZSO layer reduced the nucleation overpotential from 120 mV to 37 mV. Further, the layer extended the symmetric cell lifespan to 3800 cycles. Another study compared the dynamic behaviors of Zn electrodeposition on bare and coated Zn surfaces [277]. Fig. 12 compares the electrochemical stability and surface morphology of bare Zn and AEC-coated Zn (anti-corrosion elastic constraint) electrodes after cycling. Fig. 12 (a and d) illustrates the experimental setup of symmetric Zn/Zn and AEC-Zn/AEC-Zn cells. SEM images (Fig. 12(g and h)) of cycled bare Zn revealed porous, dendritic structures and deep pits up to 25 μm, indicating severe corrosion. Conversely, AEC-Zn (Fig. 12(i and j)) exhibited a dense, monolithic morphology with negligible corrosion. Li et al. [278] reported that smoother surfaces led to more uniform Zn deposition and less hydrogen evolution. Simulations demonstrated that higher surface roughness increased the local electric field and current density, thereby promoting dendrite growth and corrosion.

Lin et al. [279] analyzed the surface changes of LiMn2O4 cathodes and LiV3O8 anodes in ALIBs with either conventional or water-in-salt (WIS) electrolytes. After cycling, post-cycled SEM revealed that cathodes in the conventional electrolyte developed mossy and fibrous textures, which indicated surface damage. Contrarily, cathodes in the WIS electrolyte maintained their original smooth and facet morphology. The anodes exhibited less noticeable changes, but some surface deposits appeared when cycled in the conventional electrolyte.

In situ SEM techniques can be used in different ways for each battery technology and relate to the primary issues of each system. In LIBs and SIBs, these techniques are used to study mechanical degradation such as cracking, void propagation, and SEI evolution. In ASSBs, SEM visualizations focus on Li nucleation, dendrite growth, and the effect of microstructure of SEs. For aqueous batteries, such methods help visualize surface-dependent phenomena such as Zn plating, corrosion, and electrolyte interaction. Collectively, these techniques and their findings guide electrode design and material optimization across battery technologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges posed by in situ and operando SEM
ce:section.ce:para:
A summary of the challenges posed by in situ and operando SEM in battery studies is as follows:
1.
Model vs. Real Devices: Most in situ SEM setups have simple designs, such as nanobatteries. Such designs limit the types of materials and the number of cycles that can be analyzed. This is particularly important for aqueous batteries, where important issues such as gas production and corrosion occur at interfaces. These issues cannot be analyzed in simplified setups.
2.
Resolution Limits: SEM can provide detailed images at ∼10 nm, but cannot record changes at the atomic level. Atomic changes are important in LIBs and SIBs. To address this limitation, SEM is often combined with complementary techniques such as TEM, XRD, and XPS.
3.
Beam Sensitivity: Prolonged exposure to electrons can damage sensitive materials. Therefore, adjusting the beam parameters can assist in lowering electron exposure and allow for longer analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando transmission electron microscopy (TEM)
ce:section.ce:para: TEM is a powerful, advanced imaging technique that focuses a high-energy electron beam, typically accelerated in the range of 100–300 kV, through an ultrathin sample. The thickness of the sample is typically less than 100 nm. As electrons interact with the sample, some get scattered while others pass through it. The transmitted electrons are focused by using electromagnetic lenses to form a highly magnified image. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) enables the observation of atomic arrangements. Apart from its regular imaging functions, TEM can be integrated with various other imaging techniques to acquire crystallographic, structural, and chemical data. Crystallographic orientations are measured using coupled electron diffraction techniques. Furthermore, combining analytical techniques such as EDS and EELS facilitates elemental and chemical state mapping.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando TEM cell configuration
ce:section.ce:para: Developing a cell setup that allows alkali ion insertion inside the TEM is critical. Currently, two primary experimental approaches are available: open and liquid cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Open-cell setup
ce:section.ce:para: In the open-cell setup, electrode materials are exposed to the high vacuum inside the microscope to prevent the use of common electrolytes, which cannot resist high TEM vacuum environments and evaporate quickly. In 2010, the first in-situ nanocell battery was developed using LiCoO2 as the counter electrode, a SnO2 nanowire as the working electrode, and ILE (Fig. 13 a). This ILE exhibited key properties that made it suitable for in situ TEM studies, including a low vapor pressure that prevented the electrolyte from evaporating quickly in the high-vacuum environment of the TEM and stability under electron beam irradiation [280,281].

The development of experimental configurations has led to a practical approach using SSEs in open-cell configurations. Such methods require carefully applying a small amount of Li metal to a W needle inside an Ar-filled glove box. When exposed to O2, either from air or within the TEM chamber, the Li metal surface forms a thin Li2O layer that acts as an SSE. Although air exposure requires careful control to avoid creating an overly thick Li2O layer that can block ion movement, the resulting thin Li ion conducting layer works well as a replacement for liquid electrolytes [282,283]. He et al. [284] used this setup for SIBs. The in situ TEM experimental setup utilized a nanofactory TEM-scanning tunneling microscopy (TEM-STM) specimen holder where FeF2 nanoparticles (10–20 nm) were dispersed on a TEM half-grid with amorphous carbon support, functioning as the working electrode. A piezo-driven W probe coated with Na metal served as the counter electrode, while a thin Na2O layer formed on Na metal acted as the solid electrolyte. The assembly was conducted in an Ar-filled glovebox and transferred to the TEM column using a sealed Ar bag to prevent air exposure. During testing, a negative potential of ≥3 V was applied to the FeF2 electrode against the Na source to drive the sodiation process. Conversely, Wang et al. [285] utilized this setup for tracking Li transport, while a thin LiNxOy passivation layer on lithium served as the solid electrolyte (Fig. 13b).

Another approach involves using a FIB in a microelectromechanical system (MEMS) cell platform. Researchers have developed a solid-state cell by depositing LLTO electrolyte on the LiNi0.76Mn0.14Co0.10O2 cathode surface via pulsed laser deposition, followed by FIB preparation to fabricate a cross-sectional specimen. The setup includes Au/Pt layers as top electrodes, with a negative bias applied between the tip and Ni-rich LiNi0.76Mn0.14Co0.10O2 to extract Li ions (Fig. 13c). This configuration enables real-time atomic-scale observation of phase transitions and defect evolution during battery operation in NMC cathode material [286]. This technique can be applied to ASSBs. Gong et al. constructed a miniaturized configuration of a LiCoO2 cathode, Y and Ta-doped LLZO solid electrolyte, and gold anode assembled on a MEMS nanochip with FIB (Fig. 13d) [287]. However, open-cell designs have limitations when examining the SEI layer and polymerization of ILE under an electron beam [288].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Liquid-cell setup
ce:section.ce:para: To overcome the aforementioned limitations, a liquid-cell configuration has been introduced using a closed cell filled with liquid electrolyte between the working and counter electrodes. The liquid-cell setup was initially proposed in 2003 by dynamic observation of the nucleation and growth of Cu clusters during electrodeposition in in situ TEM [289]. However, in battery application, Meng Gu et al. developed a setup comprising two Si chips with electron-transparent silicon nitride membranes (∼50 nm thick) that encapsulated a liquid electrolyte (1.0 M LiClO4 in EC/DMC). The Si nanowires were mounted on Pt electrodes using FIB welding, while Li metal was placed on a separate electrode (Fig. 14 a). This configuration allowed for the observation of structural evolution and electrode-electrolyte interactions when the nanowire was fully immersed in an electrolyte [290].

As depicted in Fig. 14b, the latest liquid-cell configuration comprises a three-electrode micro-device integrated into a specialized TEM holder capable of accommodating aqueous electrolytes. The device is fabricated using two Si wafer chips with electron-transparent silicon nitride membranes. The working, reference, and counter electrodes are composed of Pt. The aqueous electrolyte (ZnSO4, ZnSO4/MnSO4, or Zn(CF3SO3)2) is confined by silicon nitride and controlled through a pressure-based liquid pump. This configuration enabled direct observation of Zn electrode behavior during electrochemical cycling while maintaining the high-vacuum environment necessary for TEM imaging [291].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando TEM analysis for Li-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, we examine representative electrode materials across three main categories: intercalation, alloying, and conversion. The focus is on how in situ TEM studies have highlighted changes in electrode behavior under varying test conditions or material properties. These parameters have been shown to affect phase and structural evolution, ultimately allowing for better control of reversible capacity and improved mechanical stability.

In the case of intercalation materials, olivine-type LFP is a distinguished cathode material owing to its abundant elements, cost-effectiveness, safety, lifespan, and environmental compatibility [292]. The Li insertion processes of LFP particles remain a subject of debate. Several models have been proposed: I) shrinking core model: describes the progress of the reaction front as inward during delithiation [293]. II) Mosaic model: indicates that delithiation occurs at multiple locations within a particle, creating unconverted LFP domains [294]. III) Domino-cascade model: describes the propagation of de-intercalation in a wave-like manner across the particle [295].

During recent years, different phenomena have been recognized to prove the exact mechanism of lithiation/delithiation in LFP. Fig. 15 a presents the first real-time atomic-scale observation of phase boundary migration during electrochemical lithiation of FP microparticles using in situ HRTEM. Distinct anisotropic lithiation with Li ion diffusion confined to channels along the b-axis is observed. The study reveals a sharp phase boundary between FP and LFP that aligns along the (010) plane and migrates along the [010] direction, with periodic misfit dislocations at the phase boundary facilitating elastic strain energy relaxation. These findings strongly support a two-phase transition mechanism in microsized FP particles rather than a solid-solution pathway [296].

Furthermore, in situ HRTEM is used to observe a solid solution zone (SSZ) formation in LFP electrode materials during delithiation (Fig. 15b) [297]. Researchers have discovered a Li-sublattice disordered SSZ that rapidly forms and reaches dimensions of 10–25 nm × 20–40 nm, contrary to previously reported sharp LFP|FP interfaces [296]. This SSZ remains stable for hundreds of seconds at room temperature under experimental conditions with an applied voltage of 3–4 V and an estimated charging rate of approximately 1C. Notably, the SSZ exhibits no dislocations, unlike the nanoscopically sharp LFP|FP interface, suggesting potential benefits for reduced fatigue and enhanced cycle life. Research suggests that this disordered SSZ can dominate phase transformation behavior under nonequilibrium conditions with high current/voltage application, particularly in larger particles, where it could provide an out-of-equilibrium but atomically wide path for Li+/e− transport. Lachal et al. confirmed that LFP underwent a two-phase reaction during chemical delithiation, following the "Shrinking Core" model (Fig. 15c). During delithiation, each particle developed a core of LFP surrounded by a shell of FP. The process involved narrow single-phase domains (LiαFePO4 and Li1-βFePO4) at the phase boundaries [298].

Holtz et al. [290] observed competing delithiation mechanisms occurring simultaneously in different particles, including core-shell structures and anisotropic growth patterns. The technique revealed particle-by-particle delithiation with slow nucleation and growth, contrary to the "domino cascade" model, and demonstrated significant degradation through particle fracturing. The technique utilized a custom-designed liquid flow cell with an aqueous electrolyte (0.5M Li2SO4). However, another study provided strong experimental support for the "domino-cascade model," validated through TEM with precession electron diffraction (PED) [299]. Another investigation highlighted the importance of overpotential in phase transformation mechanisms. Under overpotential conditions, the solid–solution reaction could act as the phase transformation mechanism for LFP. When overpotential was removed, the intermediate LixFePO4 phase separated into LFP and FP, typically observed as a two-phase reaction. Further, studies have focused on the novel two-step solid–solution mechanism in the LFP cathode during delithiation. The process includes both non-uniform and uniform solid-solution transformation pathways (schematic is shown in Fig. 15d) [300].

In summary, all three models have been supported by experimental analyses and theoretical calculations, depending on test conditions and material properties. Since the mechanisms underlying various phase transitions remain unclear, a comprehensive understanding requires the integration of coupled characterization techniques targeting both the bulk and interfaces, along with theoretical modeling.

Alloying-type electrode materials such as Si, Ge, and Sn are important candidates for LIBs anode material because they can achieve high theoretical capacity through the formation of Li–X alloys (X for Si, Ge, and Sn) [301]. Si is particularly notable due to its abundance and high theoretical capacity of 4200 mAh g−1. However, Si experiences significant volume change, pulverization, fracturing, and capacity degradation during repeated cycling [302–304]. In situ TEM studies have revealed several key aspects of Si anode behavior to understand the failure mechanisms.

McDowell et al. [305] reported that during the initial lithiation of amorphous silicon (a-Si), a distinct interface developed between a Li-rich shell and a Si-rich core (Fig. 16 a). Their observation suggested that the breaking of Si–Si bonds served as the rate-limiting step rather than Li diffusion. Although this mechanism shared some resemblance to the lithiation process in crystalline Si (c-Si), differences were observed in terms of reaction kinetics and mechanical behavior. The a-Si spheres exhibited several advantages over c-Si. Unlike c-Si, which experiences a slowdown at the reaction front during lithiation, a-Si maintains a constant lithiation rate. Further, a-Si exhibits much greater resistance to fracture. Above diameters of 150 nm, c-Si begins to fracture, but spheres up to 870 nm remain intact. The improved mechanical stability of a-Si can be attributed to numerous reasons, including lower concentration of Li in the lithiated phase, different stress evolution patterns, and absence of the c-Si anisotropic expansion effects. Interestingly, after the first lithiation cycle, the material tends to shift toward a single-phase reaction mechanism. This behavior is attributed to the presence of trapped Li or changes in the a-Si structure. Overall, these observations indicate that a-Si experiences less severe physical transformations than c-Si during the initial cycle, making it a more promising candidate for battery applications. Wang et al. confirmed the mechanisms behind electrochemically driven solid-state amorphization during the lithiation of crystalline Si using a combination of in situ TEM-EELS and molecular dynamics simulations [306].

Following the analysis of various Si morphologies, the anisotropic swelling of Si nanowires has been directly observed during lithiation. As shown in Fig. 16b, microstructural changes were captured in a Si nanowire with a <112> growth direction during lithiation. Upon contact with the Li2O/Li electrode and applying a −2 V potential, lithiation proceeded both axially and radially. This transformed the c-Si nanowire into an amorphous Li-Si alloy (a-LixSi), which eventually crystallized into the c-Li15Si4 phase after full lithiation. The interface between the c-Si and the Li-Si alloy appeared atomically sharp, as indicated by the red arrow in Fig. 16b(a). The lithiation region displayed a distinct contrast with two parallel sub-wires separated by a central white line (Fig. 16b(a,b)) and a central crack along the axis (Fig. 16b(c)). A cross-sectional view of the lithiation-performed nanowire revealed a dumbbell-like shape (Fig. 16b(d)), with the greatest swelling occurring in the radial <110> directions and the least in the radial <111> directions [307].

A similarly sharp interface was reported [308] at ∼1 nm thickness between crystalline Si (c-Si) and the amorphous LixSi alloy (Fig. 16c). In this case, lithiation proceeded through a ledge-based mechanism, with Li atoms migrating laterally along the <110> and <112> directions within the densely packed {111} planes, leading to a layer-by-layer peeling of the {111} atomic facets. The process exhibited strong orientation dependence, with interfaces aligned along {110} and {112} planes showing significantly higher mobility compared to {111} interfaces. The differences led to anisotropic swelling behavior. The discovery of this ledge-based lithiation mechanism provided a fundamental understanding of the anisotropic swelling behavior, phase transformation, and mechanical degradation in materials during cycling [308,309]. Furthermore, this anisotropic swelling has been reported in Si pillars [310,311].

Moreover, the electrolyte environment affects the expansion of Si NPs. In LiPF6, the expansion rate was 164 %; in LiTFSI, the rate was 102 %, and in LiODFB, the rate was 96 %. In LiPF6 environments, significant particle expansion was accompanied by SEI layer formation and rupture, while LTIFSI and LIODFB electrolytes primarily exhibited SEI layer formation without substantial expansion. Further, the volume expansion of the Si particles was quantified using deep learning [312].

To investigate the pulverization of Si anode, Liu et al. discussed the effect of particle size on the lithiation behavior of Si nanoparticles (SiNPs). The researchers discovered that SiNPs smaller than ∼150 nm maintained structural integrity during initial lithiation, while larger particles developed surface cracks and ultimately fractured. The size-dependent fracture behavior was attributed to the interplay between particle diameter and crack size, where smaller particles could not store sufficient strain energy to propagate cracks, even under high-stress conditions, thereby assisting in designing more durable Si-based LIB anodes [313,314].

The most recent research has focused on developing new solutions to the critical issues with silicon-based anodes, such as volumetric expansion, structural collapse, and poor electronic conductivity. A study used operando SAXS with TEM analysis on Si anodes. The findings indicated that wrapping SiNPs with reduced graphene oxide (rGO) proved useful in controlling expansion to 182 % during cycling as opposed to 300 % in unwrapped particles (with preserved structural integrity) [315]. In another study, Si NPs were doped with CrSi2, leading to strong electrochemical performance, with an initial capacity of 1729.3 mAh g−1 at 0.2C and a retained capacity of 1085 mAh g−1 after 500 cycles. In situ TEM revealed that the Si-8 %Cr sample maintained its structural integrity during lithiation. Additionally, the Si-8 %Cr NPs demonstrated superior lithiation capability compared to pristine Si (p-Si) and Si-18 %Cr NPs. These improvements attribute to the optimized amount of CrSi2 nanocrystals, which have low electrochemical reactivity, along with improved Li ion diffusion and increased electronic conductivity resulting from CrSi2 doping (nanocrystal domains in Si-8 %Cr NPs and cracks in p-Si are shown in Fig. 16d) [316]. Together, these findings mark a significant step forward in the development of Si anodes for next-generation energy storage systems.

Apart from materials discussed thus far, several studies have used in situ TEM to investigate the behavior of conversion-type materials. Redox reactions in such materials typically occur in two distinct regions: near the surface and within the interior. While interior reactions play a major role in determining battery capacity, they require more time to initiate. Conversely, reactions close to the surface function similarly to supercapacitors owing to the shorter distances for ions and electrons. Thus, understanding the transition from near-surface to interior is essential to evaluate rate capability. For example, the lithiation mechanism in NiO has multiple stages [317–319]. Fig. 17 a(A–C) illustrate the reaction pathways and structural evolution from near-surface to interior during the lithiation of NiO nanosheets. These figures highlight two main reaction modes: the shrinking-core mode and the finger mode. In the shrinking-core mode, surface wetting occurs rapidly via surface diffusion. The redox front moves from the outer surface after near-surface sites are saturated. However, accessing the interior via this mode is slow. Conversely, finger mode involves the nucleation and growth of lithiation fingers that occur heterogeneously throughout the particles. These fingers nucleate on ⟨112⟩ facets randomly after the near-surface sites become saturated and elongate until they emerge on the opposite surface of the NiO nanosheet. Eventually, they thicken into the nanosheet bulk. Bulk mode then occurs after the fingers have thickened and merged, enhancing long-range transport through the nanosheets. Combining 3D STEM and EELS mapping of NiO fingers proves the non-uniform nature of the lithiation process and the presence of both metallic Ni0 and Li + at the sample surface and within the finger regions [317]. The finger mode propagates at an average speed significantly faster than the shrinking-core mode. Notably, there is an incubation time of approximately 100s before a finger nucleates. To improve rate performance, bypassing the slow shrinking-core mode, minimizing the incubation time associated with the faster finger mode, and strategically managing internal stress are essential. Optimizing particle design is also essential to enable rapid and complete redox reactions throughout the bulk material, even under high charge–discharge rates. This two-step lithiation process was first discussed for ZnO [320] with nanocracks in nanowires, but in MoO3 [321], CuO [322], and SnO2 [280] only the shrinking-core mode is discussed.

Phase transformations in conversion-type materials can involve intercalation and alloying reactions. For instance, He et al. [323] performed in-situ strain-sensitive bright-field STEM (BF-STEM) and demonstrated that the process initially involved an intercalation reaction and then underwent a conversion reaction. As shown in Fig. 17b, observations revealed that the initial Li intercalation into the Fe3O4 nanoparticles proceeded via a two-phase reaction sequence, forming a Li-inserted LixFe3O4 phase (lighter contrast in the figures). With continued lithiation, a conversion reaction occurred, resulting in the coexistence of three distinct phases within the nanoparticles: pristine Fe3O4, the Li-inserted LixFe3O4 (rocksalt structure), and a final composite comprising ultrafine metallic Fe nanoparticles embedded in an amorphous Li2O matrix. In situ BF-STEM imaging confirmed that both intercalation and conversion reactions proceed with the shrinking-core mode. The study integrated ex-situ synchrotron X-ray diffraction (SXRD), DFT calculations, and EELS to support their findings.

MoO3, a conversion-type electrode material, has been investigated. In situ TEM, coupled with EELS and electron diffraction pattern (EDP), provides direct observation of electrochemical lithiation/delithiation of α-MoO3 nanobelts and the corresponding microstructural changes due to capacity change during cycling. In the first lithiation, the single-crystal α-MoO3 nanobelt irreversibly transforms into a mixture of Mo nanograins embedded within a crystalline Li2O matrix with a significant volume expansion. Upon subsequent delithiation, the structure experiences a two-stage phase transition process: first, crystalline Mo nanograins transform into crystalline Li1.66Mo0.66O2, which then convert into amorphous Li2MoO3. The irreversible conversion (MoO3 → Mo → Li1.66Mo0.66O2 → Li2MoO3) is responsible for ∼68 % of the total capacity loss (∼568 mAh g−1 out of the original 1117 mAh g−1) during the first cycle. The remaining 32 % capacity loss results from predominantly irreversible side reactions such as SEI formation. After this initial irreversible cycle, a highly reversible phase transition between amorphous Li2MoO3 and crystalline Mo is observed with the repeated formation and disappearance of a crystalline Li2O layer [321].

In situ bending experiments conducted inside TEM present significant differences in the mechanical properties of pristine and fully lithiated α-MoO3 nanobelts. Pristine α-MoO3 is inherently brittle, but lithiated α-MoO3 exhibits enhanced ductility, capable of undergoing large deformation (up to ∼11 % bending strain) without cracking [324].

The final category includes conversion-alloying type materials, which have been studied in electrode materials such as ZnO and SnO2. [325] Although ZnO exhibits a similar lithiation reaction to SnO2, it undergoes a two-step lithiation process accompanied by cracks [320]. During lithiation, ZnO first converts into metallic Zn and Li2O. In the second step, Zn reacts further with Li to form a LixZn alloy (0 ≤ x ≤ 1.1).

In situ TEM has demonstrated that lithiation in single-crystalline ZnO nanowires initiates through discrete crack nucleation rather than continuous deformation or dislocation activities (Fig. 17c). These cracks propagate ahead of the lithiation front, causing the formation of multiple segmented domains that are subsequently transformed into nano-amorphous or nano-glass regions through rapid Li+ diffusion along crack surfaces, resulting in glass–glass interfaces (GGIs). These GGIs, as structural defects that affect material performance during multiple charging and discharging cycles. This leads to poor long-term performance and reduced capacity due to mechanical damage and brittleness caused by Li embrittlement [320]. Additionally, embedding ZnO quantum dots within flexible amorphous carbon sheets significantly mitigates the formation of large Zn crystals during lithiation. This approach encourages the formation of favorable LiZn alloys instead, leading to improved reversible capacity and enhanced mechanical stability in ZnO-based electrodes [326].

As one of the first electrode materials examined using in situ TEM, SnO2 is one of the most extensively studied metal oxides with this technique. The lithiation process in SnO2 nanowires begins with an initial irreversible reduction of SnO2 into nanocrystalline Sn and amorphous Li2O. Further, the battery operates through a reversible alloying reaction between Sn and Li, forming LixSn (0 ≤ x ≤ 4.4) [280]. SnO2 undergoes large anisotropic volume expansion and structural changes upon lithiation, which are directly observed at the atomic scale using TEM techniques. In situ observations show that Li ions intercalate into SnO2 in an orientation-dependent manner, inducing anisotropic expansion and generating mobile dislocations at the moving reaction fronts. This leads to the formation of a distinct “Medusa zone” with a high density of dislocations resulting from in-plane stress [280,327].

High current density has a significant impact on the formation of Sn particles during lithiation. When the current density exceeds a critical threshold of ∼20 A cm−2, the typical alloying reaction that forms LixSn is suppressed. Instead, Sn atoms rapidly agglomerate into large nanoparticles. This unusual growth is prompted by Joule heating and can pose significant safety hazards, such as the risk of short-circuiting from particles or whisker growth, in addition to accelerating capacity loss due to active material depletion. Hence, managing the current density is critical to controlling the morphology and growth of Sn crystals in the SnO2 anodes, which determines the performance and safety of the battery [328]. The introduction of graphene or carbon coatings on SnO2 nanostructures significantly affected the Li + diffusion kinetics and structural stability by enhancing electrical conductivity and mechanically confining the large volume changes during lithiation, thus suppressing the formation of dislocations and large Sn particles [329].

Lithiation behavior depends on the size of the particles. In the case of ultra-small SnO2 nanoparticles of size 15–40 nm, lithiation occurs via initial intercalation followed by a conversion-alloying reaction. This process leads to the formation of distinct acorn-shaped structures comprising a Sn/Li13Sn5 shell and a Li2O core, along with characteristic lithiation stripes. These features significantly enhance Li+ transport and rate capability [330].

ZnFe2O4 NPs display similar size-dependent behavior. Larger particles (∼40 nm) undergo a two-phase intercalation reaction with an intermediate rock-salt phase. Contrarily, smaller particles (6–9 nm) undergo a direct solid-solution transformation due to interfacial energy being more favorable in the case of smaller NPs, requiring relatively lower energy barriers to reaction kinetics and stable morphological changes. The transformation typically follows a core-shell mechanism along the {111} crystal planes [331]. Further, size-dependent lithiation behavior has been observed in NiO nanoparticles. Highly dispersed nanocubes exhibit significantly smaller expansion (∼56 %) compared to typical aggregated structures (∼100 %) due to efficient Li+ diffusion along highly exposed crystal facets like (111). These nanocubes form ultrafine Ni grains during lithiation, which stabilize the SEI layer, improve cycling stability, and enhance capacity performance [332].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ and operando TEM analysis for Na-ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: In situ TEM analysis has been used to compare the behavior of different electrode materials when Li and Na are utilized. Similar to LIBs, the mechanism of Na+ storage can be classified into three categories: intercalation, alloying, and conversion [333].

Among intercalation-type materials, HC, comprising randomly arranged and curved graphene platelets, shows noticeable structural expansion during sodiation [334]. Three main ways to store Na in HC have been suggested: adsorption, nanopore filling, and intercalation [335]. Wang et al. proposed an "absorption-to-intercalation" mechanism. Initially, Na+ absorption occurred on defects and surfaces, followed by significant volume expansion as Na+ intercalated between curved graphene layers at voltages below 0.15 V [334].

For α-MnO2 nanowires, the initial sodiation process involved Na+ intercalation into tunnels, forming an intermediate Na0.5MnO2 phase before transforming irreversibly into Mn2O3 and Na2O. Subsequent cycles revealed reversible conversion between Na0.5MnO2 and Mn2O3, but rapid capacity fading due to accumulated insulating Na2O. A comparative study with lithiation revealed that MnO2 tunnels remained stable to deeper lithiation (up to Mn3+), highlighting a stronger structural disruption caused by larger Na+ compared to Li+ [336].

Black phosphorus (BP) is another notable candidate for SIBs [337]. Interestingly, BP demonstrates orientation-dependent and anisotropic intercalation pathways (Fig. 18 a(A and B)) [338]. Li+ ions intercalate along both zigzag [96] and armchair [010] directions, resulting in uniform structural expansion. Conversely, Na+ ions diffuse strictly along zigzag channels. The energy barriers along the armchair direction are significantly higher. As shown in Fig. 18a(B), this leads to the formation of distinct columnar reaction fronts. EELS analyses confirm distinct phase evolutions. During lithiation, BP converts into Li3P through LixP intermediates. During sodiation, BP transforms into Na3P via Na3P11 and NaP intermediates. These processes indicate profound chemical and structural transformations controlled by ionic size differences.

Considering alloying-type materials, in situ TEM studies of bismuth nanosheets (Bi NSs) have revealed a multistep Na+ insertion mechanism [339]. The reaction proceeds from pristine Bi to the intermediate NaBi phase, followed by the formation of a metastable cubic Na3Bi, and finally transitions to the stable hexagonal Na3Bi phase. Fig. 18b(A) presents the HRTEM images and corresponding FFT patterns that capture the phase evolution during sodiation. Further, the morphology evolution during sodiation/desodiation of Bi NSs is shown in Fig. 18b(B). These images demonstrate that the nanosheets maintain good structural stability with no visible fractures. The intermediate cubic Na3Bi phase plays a key role in buffering the substantial volume expansion, allowing smoother transitions between phases. Bi NSs exhibit minimal volume expansion (∼13 %) compared to bulk Bi [340,341]. Li and Na insertions into Bi2Te3 both follow a similar two-step reaction mechanism involving sequential conversion and alloying processes. However, significant kinetic differences are observed with in situ TEM. Na insertion exhibits anomalously faster kinetics than Li insertion. This enhanced Na+ kinetics originates from lower interfacial strain accommodation energy and higher electrochemo-mechanical stress concentration at the concave-shaped reaction front, facilitating rapid phase transformation and ion diffusion [342]. Lastly, for conversion-type materials such as NiO, the sodiation process is different from lithiation. He et al. reported that only the shrinking-core mode occurred, which was characterized by a layer-by-layer reaction that produced a thick and crystalline Na2O surface layer [343].

AS the mechanical properties of electrode materials differ substantially during lithiation and sodiation, comparative in situ TEM studies show marked differences in the behavior of ZnO. ZnO undergoes brittle failure during lithiation and is prone to crack-driven degradation. Sodiated ZnO is more ductile. The mechanical response is attributed to high-density dislocation plasticity, which enhances structural integrity with Na insertion [344].

Insights obtained from in situ TEM have been pivotal in informing advancements aimed at refining the architecture of electrodes. Although the same materials are commonly used for both LIBs and SIBs, changes to the design must be made because sodiation and lithiation are fundamentally different processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ/operando TEM analysis for all-solid-state batteries
ce:section.ce:para: In situ TEM on ASSBs reveals important details about processes such as Li deposition/stripping, dendrite growth, interface behavior, and solid electrolyte changes. Among these, Li deposition/stripping is a key challenge and a common cause of failure. However, we can address this issue by using better host materials, adding nucleation seeds, adjusting the intrinsic properties of the materials, and designing improved structures.

Yan et al. [255] demonstrated Li nucleation dependence on the substrate. They found zero nucleation barriers on substrates with significant Li solubility, such as Au, Ag, and Mg. Conversely, substrates with low Li solubility, including Cu, Ni, C, Sn, and Si, showed considerable nucleation overpotential. This finding enabled selective and spatially controlled Li deposition. Using seeded substrates and encapsulating Li within C spheres effectively suppressed dendrite formation. Consequently, cycling stability improved with a Coulombic efficiency of 98 % over 300 cycles. Lan et al. [345] studied Li nucleation employing Au NPs embedded into amorphous C nanotubes (aCNTs). Their research revealed the reversible two-step conversion between Au and Li3Au phases, and showed a “front-growth” mechanism of spatially confined Li deposition/stripping. As depicted in Fig. 19 a(A), Li first nucleated at the Au particles within the aCNTs, and subsequent growth was confined within the nanotube structure. Thus, this effectively prevented the formation of external dendrites. During stripping, Li was reversibly removed from the same growth front. This indicated plating/stripping behavior that was well-controlled. Conversely, Fig. 19a(B) shows that in the absence of Au seeds, Li deposits uncontrollably outside the nanotubes, which leads to significant dendritic growth. These results highlight the crucial role of Au seeds in enabling spatially confined and controlled lithium deposition. Zhou et al. [346] offered valuable insights into Li diffusion dynamics by visualizing dendrite-free lithium transport through engineered atomic channels within bulk graphite. Their results demonstrated that the presence of enhanced bulk diffusion pathways significantly suppressed dendrite formation.

Another strategy involves refining rational morphology design. Liu et al. [347] used in situ TEM to study Li deposition on crumpled graphene balls with a Li2O solid electrolyte. They pointed out how well these frameworks functioned as scaffolds for dendrite-free Li growth. Initially, Li deposition occurred inside the graphene particles, while later it occurred without dendritic growth. The crumpled structure, with its uniformly distributed meso/macropores, effectively accommodated Li and prevented dendritic growth. Additionally, this structure showed minimal volume fluctuation during cycling. Consequently, a high Coulombic efficiency of 97.5 % over 750 cycles and stable Li deposition up to 12 mAh cm−2 without dendritic morphology were observed.

The whisker-like Li dendrite formation mechanism, growth, and mechanical stresses have been studied in depth through the integration of in situ TEM with AFM. Zhang et al. [348] utilized an innovative experimental setup combining an AFM cantilever tip modified with a CNT as the counter electrode, a Li metal electrode, and a naturally formed Li2CO3 solid electrolyte layer within an environmental TEM chamber (Fig. 19b(a–b)). As shown in Fig. 19b(c), Li whiskers first nucleated as spherical particles at the interface between the Li electrode and Li2CO3 solid electrolyte. These particles subsequently elongated into rod-like whiskers. The whiskers preferentially grew along specific crystallographic directions, such as <111> and <112>, following a root-growth mechanism. The growth was driven by electrochemical overpotentials and diffusion-controlled processes. AFM measurements indicated that Li whiskers generated compressive stresses of up to 130 MPa, substantially greater than previously reported for micrometer [349] or bulk-scale Li [350]. Further mechanical tests conducted on whiskers under no applied voltage demonstrated yield strengths of 12.2–244 MPa, suggesting a strong size-dependent strengthening effect relative to bulk Li. These findings offer important benchmarks for designing strategies to prevent Li dendrite growth in ASSBs or control the cracks in electrolytes. For example, the fracture stress of Li0.33La0.567TiO3 ranges from 150 to 250 MPa [351–353], Li1.3Al0.3Ti1.7(PO4)3 is around 150 MPa [354,355], and Li7La3Zr2O12 is approximately 280 MPa [355].

Further investigations into Li whisker formation have highlighted the critical role of SEI. Whisker nucleation starts with single-crystalline Li particle formation, which tends to terminate on low-energy {110} faces. These observations indicate that the primary driving force for nucleation is surface energy. Changes from isotropic lithium particle growth to directional whisker growth are attributed to sluggish Li ion transport through the SEI layer. This slow transport results in localized Li deposition at the whisker-electrolyte interface. The composition of SEI quite significantly determines the morphology and the rate of whisker growth. Particularly, SEI layers formed in CO2-rich environments substantially influence these properties. Further, various stress-related behaviors of Li whiskers under mechanical loading have been observed, such as buckling, growth cessation, kinking, and mechanical yielding. Such behaviors are strongly dependent on whisker geometry, applied potentials, and local stress conditions. Notably, experiments that simulate constraints similar to those found in battery separators reveal that mechanical confinement can dramatically alter whisker growth dynamics and stability. These findings emphasize the need to carefully engineer both mechanical and chemical interfaces to improve the performance and safety of Li-based battery systems [356].

Besides the formation of Li dendrites, the interaction between alkali metals and environmental gases is crucial for their stability in energy storage. Li et al. [282] used in situ ETEM to study the degradation of Li and Na metals in dry air and oxygen. Li forms a compact Li2O passivation layer with minimal growth. Conversely, Na experiences severe oxidation, creating a rough layer of Na2O/Na2O2 due to the Kirkendall effect. SAED and EELS show that the passivation layer of Li contains Li2O and Li2CO3, while Na is covered by Na2CO3. When CO2 is present, uniform layers of carbonate are observed, with Na2CO3 forming fast and Li2CO3 growing gradually. In pure oxygen (O2), Li creates a thin layer of Li2O, while Na forms uneven structures of Na2O/Na2O2. Notably, pre-treating Na with CO2 assists in fabricating a stable Na2CO3 layer, which prevents further degradation in dry air and an artificial SEI can stabilize Na metals.

As part of efforts to investigate key parameters in ASSBs, Nomura et al. [357] discussed the critical role of SEs. Using time-resolved EELS, they provided dynamic Li mapping and valuable insights into Li transport within the solid electrolyte Li1+x + yAlx(Ti,Ge)2−xSiyP 3−yO12 (LASGTP). Their findings revealed that Li diffusion was influenced by the interface between electrolyte/electrode, with Li ions migrating preferentially from regions closer to the LASGTP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ/operando TEM analysis for aqueous batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Most studies using in situ TEM on aqueous batteries have focused on Zn-based systems. A key area of research interest is the formation and growth of Zn dendrites. These dendrites can cause short circuits or reduce battery performance [42,273,358].

Zeng et al. [359] used a 0.02 M solution of zinc sulfate (with a pH of 5.51) in liquid-phase cells for the formation of shape-controlled zinc nanosheets. HADDF-STEM confirmed that Zn ions gather near the electrode before deposition begins, and this buildup is crucial for the process. Zn tends to grow into 2D hexagonal sheets rather than reducing surface energy. The growth happens through epitaxial or stacking mechanisms, not random aggregation. While Zeng et al. studied Zn deposition, Sasaki et al. [360] investigated how Zn dissolves using the same electrolyte. They found that Zn first formed hexagonal plate-like crystals with {10-10} facets. Then, it grows into dendrites in the <11–20> directions with 60° branching angles (Fig. 20 a). During the dissolution process, the roots of the dendrites dissolve faster than their tips because of concentration differences. This leads to detachment and loss of capacity. Both studies show that local ion concentration, electric field distribution, and surface energy greatly affect Zn electrodeposited morphology. These observations at the nanoscale are crucial for developing Zn anodes that do not form dendrites, which is important for the next generation of battery technologies.

Researchers have investigated the behavior of Zn plating/stripping in different water-based electrolytes [291]. Problems such as zinc dendrites, dead zinc, and hydrogen evolution limit are analyzed, and their influence on the stability of ZnSO4 solutions is discussed. To improve stability, they added Mn2+ to the solution. This caused Mn-based clusters to form, which helped control the deposition of Zn2+. This method suppressed dendrite formation and increased cycling stability. Using a Zn(CF3SO3)2 solution helped create a dendrite-free anode by changing the Zn2+ solvation structure. This change reduced hydrogen evolution and promoted more uniform Zn plating. Molecular dynamics simulations showed that the bulky CF3SO3 − anions helped reduce the effects of Zn2+ solvation. This enhancement increased the charge transfer kinetics and stabilized the electrode surface.

To improve battery performance, better electrolytes should be designed along with controlling dendrites. A major issue with water-based electrolytes is their narrow electrochemical stability window of 1.23 V. This limited range can cause hydrogen and oxygen to form, which results in poor cycling stability and lower energy density [70]. To address this limitation, water-in-salt (WiS) [70] and water-in-bisalt (WiBS) [361] electrolyte systems have been developed, and their structural and chemical evolution has been examined using in situ TEM. For example, a 21 m LiTFSI-based WiS electrolyte enabled the operation of a 2.3 V ALIBs with a LiMn2O4/Mo6S8 configuration. This system reached almost 100 % coulombic efficiency after 1000 cycles. Further improvements were achieved with WiBS electrolytes. X and colleagues used lithium trifluoromethane sulfonate (LiOTf) as lithium salt. This salt helped to form a compact SEI, which allowed the integration of a TiO2 anode. As a result, the voltage increased to 2.5 V, and the energy density reached 100 Wh/kg.

While WiS strategies have improved the stability of the electrolyte, Shang et al. [40] introduced an alternative approach using an ether-in-water electrolyte (EIWE) to further enhance SEI formation. Within this system, LiTFSI was dissolved into tetraethylene glycol dimethyl ether (TEGDME) at different molar ratios. Fig. 20b(a–f) present in situ TEM observations of electrode surface morphologies in a full cell using a Li4Ti5O12 anode and a LiMn2O4 cathode under different electrolyte conditions, including untreated, 9 m EIWE, and 15 m EIWE. These images offer critical insights into the formation mechanisms of SEI and CEI. The results indicate that higher LiTFSI concentrations in the EIWE lead to the development of more uniform and protective interfacial films on both electrodes.

The role of TEM varies for different battery technologies. In LIBs and SIBs, it shows how ion insertion affects volume expansion, particle fracture, and loss of capacity. In ASSBs, in situ TEM assists in elucidating methods to reduce Li dendrites and improve the interface. For aqueous systems, TEM emphasizes how electrolyte composition can reduce side reactions and enhance reversibility. Providing detailed information on morphology, phase changes, and ion diffusion pathways, it helps researchers design better electrodes and electrolytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges of in situ and operando TEM
ce:section.ce:para:
The main challenges of in situ and operando TEM are as follows:
1.
Beam Damage: High-energy electron beams can affect on electrochemical behavior, unwanted side reactions, and sensitive materials. Strategies such as low-dose imaging [362], cryo-TEM [363], “direct electron detector” [364], and 4D-STEM [365] are essential for minimizing damage and preserving data accuracy.
2.
Temporal Resolution and Data Management: Processing large datasets with low doses of data requires using artificial intelligence (AL) and machine learning (ML) tools to reduce noise, sort data, and analyze information on their own.
3.
Resolution limitations in liquid-cell setups: The presence of liquid reduces spatial resolution due to electron scattering and absorption. Although the thickness and transparency of the window materials affect resolution, technological improvements such as using thinner SiNx or graphene windows are still required [366].
4.
Interface and Interphase Characterization: analyzing SEI/CEI formation, particularly in Li-metal and solid-state systems, remains difficult due to beam sensitivity and dynamic changes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion and perspective
ce:section.ce:para: A thorough understanding of electrode and electrolyte behavior in various rechargeable batteries is crucial for developing next-generation energy storage systems. Real-time investigations provide insights into the connections between failure mechanisms and the dynamic changes of active materials and interfacial structures.

Recent studies using in situ and operando XRD and TEM have improved our understanding of how lithium ions (Li+) are inserted into LIBs. These studies focus on three main processes: intercalation, alloying, and conversion reactions. They show how each process affects critical aspects of battery performance, such as capacity, reaction kinetics, and reversibility. Furthermore, the dynamic behavior of electrode materials has been shown to depend on parameters such as particle size, carbon composition, C-rate, temperature, synthesis method, and cation composition. For example, incorporating Mn cations into layered cathodes promotes a two-phase transition, which can reduce ion transport kinetics. These findings offer useful strategies for future development. Li-rich layered oxides (such as Li-rich NMC) can deliver exceptional capacities (300 mAh g−1) [367] and reduce the cost per kilowatt-hour. Additionally, layered 2D metal chalcogenides, which are bonded by van der Waals forces, facilitate easy ion insertion. During cycling, these materials can experience all intercalation, conversion, and alloying reactions. In situ X-ray-based techniques, including XRD-CT and XPS, have further revealed degradation by providing direct observation of SEI evolution and oxygen loss. These findings suggest tuning the electrolyte composition or additive selection to mitigate side reactions. To meet the ambitious target of achieving 500 Wh kg−1 energy density, Li metal and Si anodes are among the most promising candidates. In this context, we have guided in situ SEM to visualize crack formation and void generation caused by considerable volume changes. As a result, nano-engineered Si structures and Si composites are currently being explored to better accommodate this swelling.

For SIBs, experiments performed in real-time allow the comparison of how Na and Li ions behave. While both Li+ and Na+ share some similarities in their electrochemical behavior, there exist important differences. For example, Na+ ions mainly diffuse through zigzag channels, while Li+ ions can move through both zigzag and armchair paths. Additionally, Na+ tends to move faster during insertion. Conversely, MnO2 nanowires are more stable when undergoing deep lithiation than during sodiation. These findings emphasize the need to modify Li-based electrodes for SIBs by using doping and surface coating [368]. Researchers should focus on creating novel structures and morphologies specifically for SIBs to achieve higher energy densities.

In ASSBs, in situ X-ray techniques have been utilized to study the phase and interface evolution of SSEs. Research on sulfide-based glass-ceramic electrolytes, such as Li2S–P2S5 exhibits no peak shifts during in situ XRD, indicating structural stability and improved long-term performance. Future research should prioritize advanced electrolyte systems, especially sulfide-based and high-entropy halide electrolytes that enhance ionic conductivity and stability. Imaging techniques such as TEM, SEM, and XRT reveal Li distribution heterogeneities in electrode bulk and interfacial phenomena, including voids, Li whiskers, and dendrites. For example, in situ XRT captures how changes at the SSE interface contribute to overall cell shrinkage and decreased stack pressure during cycling. For practical ASSB applications, future developments should aim for electrodes and electrolytes that work well without requiring extra pressure [369]. Improving discharge protocols assists in managing interfacial processes [370], making it easier to use such batteries in EVs, with liquid or solid electrolytes.

Research on aqueous batteries is focused on plating/stripping behavior, interfacial properties, and dendrite formation in various electrolyte compositions. As observed through in situ TEM images, adding Li salts to LIB electrolytes can enhance the SEI layer. In addition, higher LiTFSI concentration in EIWE leads to uniform interfacial films on both electrodes. Such an understanding opens new avenues for engineering complex electrolytes, along with new concepts such as asymmetric electrolytes [371]. Another approach involves adjusting the pH of the electrolytes [372].

To further enhance analysis capabilities, several emerging techniques are now being explored. Emerging in situ and operando tools are contributing significantly to battery research by enabling more precise and multidimensional analyses. Recent advancements in synchrotron sources have enabled the deployment of next-generation techniques such as High-Energy Diffraction Microscopy (HEDM) and Bragg Coherent Diffraction Imaging (BCDI), which provide grain-resolved and intragranular mapping with improved spatial and temporal resolution. These capabilities are supported by advancements in detector technologies and optical systems. Additionally, X-ray photoemission electron microscopy (XPEEM) and wide-angle X-ray scattering (WAXS) are emerging as valuable techniques for observing interfaces in lithium-based batteries and for probing microscopic solvation structures, respectively. The combination of spectroscopy with scattering or diffraction methods further enhances the ability to investigate both long-range and short-range structural order. Cross-disciplinary integration with AI and ML is also accelerating data processing, image analysis, and information extraction, supporting the development of more efficient and insightful electrochemical in situ characterization approaches.
